<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are "Types of Elements" in FEM?

In the Finite Element Method (FEM), a **continuum (the body or domain under analysis)** is discretized into a finite number of smaller, well-defined sub-regions known as **Finite Elements**. These elements are connected to each other at discrete points called **nodes**. The classification of these elements based on their **dimensionality**, **geometric shape**, **number of nodes**, and the **order of interpolation** of their shape functions is collectively referred to as the **Types of Elements**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A *Finite Element* is a small piece of a structure or continuum over which the unknown field variable (displacement, temperature, pressure, etc.) is approximated by simple interpolation polynomials using the nodal values as unknowns. The "type" of an element is characterized by its **dimensionality** (1D, 2D, 3D), **geometry** (bar, beam, triangle, quadrilateral, tetrahedron, brick), and the **polynomial order** (linear, quadratic, cubic) of its shape (interpolation) functions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Building with LEGO Bricks

Imagine that you are asked to estimate the area of an irregularly shaped land plot for a construction project. Instead of trying to apply a single complex integration formula over the entire weird shape, you overlay the plot with a **mesh of simple geometric tiles** (triangles or rectangles). You can now compute the area of each simple tile easily and sum them up to get a very accurate estimate of the total area.

- The **irregular land plot** = the continuous physical domain (beam, plate, 3D solid).
- The **simple tiles** = **finite elements** (triangles, quadrilaterals, bricks, tetrahedra).
- The **corners where tiles meet** = **nodes** (where the unknown field is solved).
- The **size of each tile** = **element size** (refinement → smaller tiles → better accuracy).

The "type of element" therefore dictates the **shape of these tiles** (triangle vs quadrilateral vs brick) and the **complexity of the math used inside each tile** (linear vs quadratic interpolation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** The choice of element type is NOT arbitrary. It directly controls:
> - **Accuracy** of the solution (higher-order elements give better results per element).
> - **Computational cost** (more nodes per element = larger stiffness matrices).
> - **Mesh generation ease** (triangles are easiest for complex geometries).
> - **Convergence rate** to the true physical solution as the mesh is refined.

---

## 1.3 Primary Classification Axes

Finite elements can be broadly classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

| Classification Axis | Categories |
|---|---|
| **By Dimensionality** | 1-D, 2-D, 3-D elements |
| **By Geometry** | Bars, Beams, Triangles, Quadrilaterals, Tetrahedra, Bricks, Wedges |
| **By Polynomial Order of Shape Function** | Linear (L), Quadratic (Q), Cubic (C) |
| **By Parameterization Family** | Isoparametric, Sub-parametric, Super-parametric |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to "list and explain types of elements," always begin by stating these four classification axes. Examiners reward a structured response that demonstrates breadth before depth.

---

## 1.4 Quick Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following metrics are universally used to characterize any finite element:

- **Number of Nodes per element (n):** Determines the number of DOF.
- **Number of Displacement components per node (D):** 1 for trusses/heat, 2 for 2D solids/beams, 3 for 3D solids.
- **Total DOF per element:** $n \times D$.
- **Size of Element Stiffness Matrix $[K]^e$:** $(n \cdot D) \times (n \cdot D)$.
- **Order of Element:** Equal to the polynomial degree of the shape function minus 1 in 1D (Linear = Order 1, Quadratic = Order 2).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing the **order of the element** with the **number of nodes**. A quadratic 1D element has 3 nodes but is of *Order 2*. A cubic 1D element has 4 nodes but is of *Order 3*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 One-Dimensional (1-D) Elements

1-D elements are used to model **bars, trusses, beams, shafts, and heat conduction along a rod**.

### 2.1.1 Linear 1-D Element (Two-Node Element)

- **Nodes:** 2 (one at each end).
- **DOF per node:** 2 (for beams: $u$ and $v$ / $\theta$); 1 for trusses/heat ($u$ or $T$).
- **Shape functions** (in natural coordinate $\xi$):

$$
N_1(\xi) = \frac{1-\xi}{2}, \qquad N_2(\xi) = \frac{1+\xi}{2}
$$

where $\xi \in [-1, +1]$ is the **natural (isoparametric) coordinate**.

- **Polynomial order:** 1 (linear interpolation).
- **Total DOF (2-node truss):** 4 ($2 \text{ nodes} \times 2 \text{ DOF/node}$).
- **Stiffness matrix size:** $4 \times 4$.

### 2.1.2 Quadratic 1-D Element (Three-Node Element)

- **Nodes:** 3 (two end nodes + one mid-side node).
- **Shape functions:**

$$
N_1 = \frac{-\xi(1-\xi)}{2}, \qquad N_2 = (1-\xi)(1+\xi), \qquad N_3 = \frac{\xi(1+\xi)}{2}
$$

- **Polynomial order:** 2.
- **Advantages:** Captures bending in beams accurately; linear strain varies linearly.
- **Use case:** Curved beams, stress concentration regions.

### 2.1.3 Cubic and Higher-Order 1-D Elements

- **Cubic (4 nodes):** Used for high-precision beam analysis.
- **Lagrangian family** of elements uses equally spaced nodes and Lagrange interpolation polynomials of degree $(n-1)$ for $n$ nodes.

---

## 2.2 Two-Dimensional (2-D) Elements

2-D elements discretize plates, membranes, and plane stress/strain problems.

### 2.2.1 Triangular Elements

| Type | Nodes | DOF (plane stress) | Shape Function Order |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CST** (Constant Strain Triangle) | 3 | 6 | Linear |
| **LST** (Linear Strain Triangle) | 6 | 12 | Quadratic |
| **QST** (Quadratic Strain Triangle) | 10 | 20 | Cubic |

#### (a) CST — Constant Strain Triangle

- 3 corner nodes, each with 2 DOF ($u, v$).
- Displacement field varies **linearly** within the element.
- Strains are **constant** throughout the element → hence "constant strain."
- **Pros:** Simple, robust, ideal for complex geometries.
- **Cons:** Overly stiff (poor accuracy) due to constant strain assumption.

The shape functions in **area coordinates** $L_1, L_2, L_3$ (where $L_1 + L_2 + L_3 = 1$) are:

$$
N_1 = L_1, \qquad N_2 = L_2, \qquad N_3 = L_3
$$

### 2.2.2 Quadrilateral Elements

| Type | Nodes | DOF | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Q4** (4-node quad) | 4 (corners only) | 8 | Bilinear shape functions |
| **Q8** (8-node quad) | 8 (4 corners + 4 mid-sides) | 16 | Quadratic (serendipity) |
| **Q9** (9-node quad) | 9 (8 + 1 center) | 18 | Full quadratic (Lagrangian) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Q4 vs CST Trade-off:** Q4 elements are **more accurate per element** but **harder to mesh** on curved boundaries. CST elements are easier to mesh but require **more elements** for the same accuracy. Most commercial codes (ANSYS, ABAQUS) prefer Q4/Q8 for regular regions and CST for transitions.

---

## 2.3 Three-Dimensional (3-D) Elements

Used for solid mechanics, thermal analysis in solids, etc.

| Element | Geometry | Typical Nodes | DOF |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tetrahedron** | 4-faced solid | 4 (linear), 10 (quadratic) | 12 / 30 |
| **Hexahedron (Brick)** | 6-faced cube-like | 8 (linear), 20 (serendipity) | 24 / 60 |
| **Wedge (Prism)** | Triangular prism | 6 (linear), 15 (quadratic) | 18 / 45 |

The **20-node hexahedral (serendipity) element** is the workhorse of 3-D FEM analysis due to its excellent accuracy-to-cost ratio.

---

## 2.4 The Isoparametric Family

This is the **most important classification for KTU exams**.

| Type | Geometry Mapping Order | Displacement Mapping Order |
|---|---|---|
| **Sub-parametric** | Lower | Higher |
| **Isoparametric** | **Same** | **Same** |
| **Super-parametric** | Higher | Lower |

### 2.4.1 Isoparametric Element (Most Common)

The **same shape functions** $N_i$ used to interpolate the **geometry** are also used to interpolate the **displacement field**:

$$
x(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(\xi, \eta) \, x_i, \qquad y(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(\xi, \eta) \, y_i
$$

$$
u(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(\xi, \eta) \, u_i, \qquad v(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(\xi, \eta) \, v_i
$$

**Examples:** 2-node truss → linear bar; 3-node triangle → CST; 4-node quad → Q4; 8-node quad → Q8; 8-node brick → linear hexahedron; 20-node brick → serendipity hexahedron.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Iso"?** *Iso* means "equal." The element uses the *same* polynomial order for both geometric and field variable mapping. This produces **well-conditioned matrices** for most engineering problems and forms the backbone of every modern FEM code.

### 2.4.2 Super-parametric Element

Geometry is mapped with a **higher** polynomial order than the displacement. Used when accurate boundary description is critical.

### 2.4.3 Sub-parametric Element

Displacement is mapped with a **higher** polynomial order than the geometry. Less common in practice.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Memorization for KTU Board Exam — Types of Elements**

| # | Formula / Property | Expression | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total DOF per element | $DOF_e = n \times d$ | $n$ = nodes, $d$ = DOF/node |
| 2 | Linear 1-D shape functions | $N_1 = \tfrac{1-\xi}{2}, \ N_2 = \tfrac{1+\xi}{2}$ | 2-node bar/truss |
| 3 | Quadratic 1-D shape functions | $N_1 = \tfrac{-\xi(1-\xi)}{2}, \ N_2 = (1-\xi^2), \ N_3 = \tfrac{\xi(1+\xi)}{2}$ | 3-node bar/beam |
| 4 | CST area coordinates | $L_1 + L_2 + L_3 = 1$ | Triangular element |
| 5 | Isoparametric mapping | $x = \sum N_i x_i, \ u = \sum N_i u_i$ | All iso elements |
| 6 | Jacobian determinant | $\vert J \vert = \vert \partial(x,y) / \partial(\xi,\eta) \vert$ | Required for stiffness integration |
| 7 | Q4 stiffness size | $8 \times 8$ | 4-node quad plane stress |
| 8 | Q8 stiffness size | $16 \times 16$ | 8-node quad plane stress |
| 9 | 20-node brick stiffness size | $60 \times 60$ | 3-D solid analysis |
| 10 | Pascal's triangle order | Linear → 3-node, Quad → 6/8-node, Cubic → 10/9-node | Polynomial completeness |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (wing skin):** Uses 2D triangular (CST) and quadrilateral (Q4/Q8) membrane elements.
- **Civil (dam, bridge):** 3D 20-node brick elements for mass concrete, tetrahedra for complex rebar geometry.
- **Automotive crashworthiness:** Linear and quadratic tetrahedra (C3D4, C3D10) for explicit dynamics.
- **Electronics thermal management:** 3-D hexahedra with 8 or 20 nodes for heat conduction.
- **Biomedical (bone implants):** Higher-order tetrahedra (10-node) to capture curved anatomical surfaces.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Shape Functions of a Linear 1-D Element

The linear 1-D element is the simplest non-trivial FEM element. We will derive its shape functions from **first principles** using the **Lagrange interpolation** approach.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a straight bar element of length $L$ with two end nodes:
- Node 1 at $x = 0$ with nodal displacement $u_1$.
- Node 2 at $x = L$ with nodal displacement $u_2$.

We seek to approximate the displacement field $u(x)$ over the element using a **linear polynomial**:

$$
u(x) = a_0 + a_1 x
$$

This is a polynomial of **degree 1** with **2 unknown constants** $a_0, a_1$, which matches the **2 nodal DOFs**. This is the consistency requirement of FEM.

### 3.1.2 Apply Boundary Conditions (Nodal Values)

Substitute the nodal coordinates into the polynomial:

**At Node 1** $(x = 0)$:

$$
u(0) = u_1 \implies u_1 = a_0 + a_1 (0) = a_0
$$

**At Node 2** $(x = L)$:

$$
u(L) = u_2 \implies u_2 = a_0 + a_1 L
$$

### 3.1.3 Solve for Constants

From the first equation: $a_0 = u_1$.

Substituting into the second:

$$
u_2 = u_1 + a_1 L \implies a_1 = \frac{u_2 - u_1}{L}
$$

### 3.1.4 Express Displacement Field in Terms of Nodal Values

Substitute $a_0$ and $a_1$ back into $u(x)$:

$$
u(x) = u_1 + \left(\frac{u_2 - u_1}{L}\right) x
$$

Group terms by nodal displacements:

$$
u(x) = \left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right) u_1 + \left(\frac{x}{L}\right) u_2
$$

Define **shape functions**:

$$
N_1(x) = 1 - \frac{x}{L}, \qquad N_2(x) = \frac{x}{L}
$$

Hence:

$$
u(x) = N_1(x)\, u_1 + N_2(x)\, u_2 = \sum_{i=1}^{2} N_i(x)\, u_i
$$

### 3.1.5 Property Check (Kruskal–Krook / Kronecker Delta Property)

At Node 1 ($x = 0$): $N_1(0) = 1, \ N_2(0) = 0$ → $u(0) = u_1$ ✓
At Node 2 ($x = L$): $N_1(L) = 0, \ N_2(L) = 1$ → $u(L) = u_2$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Partition of Unity:** $N_1(x) + N_2(x) = 1 - \tfrac{x}{L} + \tfrac{x}{L} = 1$. This property must hold for *any* valid set of shape functions — it guarantees that a rigid-body translation is reproduced exactly.

### 3.1.6 Transform to Natural Coordinate $\xi$

We map the physical coordinate $x \in [0, L]$ to the natural coordinate $\xi \in [-1, +1]$ using:

$$
x = \frac{L}{2}(1 + \xi)
$$

Substituting into $N_1$:

$$
N_1 = 1 - \frac{x}{L} = 1 - \frac{1}{L} \cdot \frac{L}{2}(1+\xi) = \frac{2 - (1+\xi)}{2} = \frac{1-\xi}{2}
$$

Similarly:

$$
N_2 = \frac{x}{L} = \frac{1+\xi}{2}
$$

This yields the **standard isoparametric linear shape functions**:

$$
N_1(\xi) = \frac{1-\xi}{2}, \qquad N_2(\xi) = \frac{1+\xi}{2}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Shape Functions of a Quadratic 1-D Element (3-Node)

### 3.2.1 Setup

We use 3 nodes with the following coordinates and displacements:
- Node 1 at $x = 0$, displacement $u_1$.
- Node 2 at $x = L/2$, displacement $u_2$.
- Node 3 at $x = L$, displacement $u_3$.

The interpolation polynomial must be of degree 2 (to match 3 DOFs):

$$
u(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2
$$

### 3.2.2 Boundary Conditions

$$
u_1 = a_0
$$

$$
u_2 = a_0 + a_1 \tfrac{L}{2} + a_2 \tfrac{L^2}{4}
$$

$$
u_3 = a_0 + a_1 L + a_2 L^2
$$

### 3.2.3 Solve the System (Lagrange Form — Direct)

Rather than solving algebraically, we use the **Lagrange interpolation formula** to write each shape function directly. For node $i$ with coordinate $x_i$, the shape function is:

$$
N_i(x) = \prod_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} \frac{x - x_j}{x_i - x_j}
$$

For $N_1$ (drop terms involving $x_1$ itself):

$$
N_1(x) = \frac{(x - x_2)(x - x_3)}{(x_1 - x_2)(x_1 - x_3)} = \frac{(x - L/2)(x - L)}{(0 - L/2)(0 - L)} = \frac{(x - L/2)(x - L)}{(L^2 / 2)}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
N_1(x) = \frac{2(x - L/2)(x - L)}{L^2} = \frac{2}{L^2}\left(x^2 - \tfrac{3L}{2}x + \tfrac{L^2}{2}\right)
$$

$$
N_1(x) = \frac{2x^2}{L^2} - \frac{3x}{L} + 1
$$

For $N_2$ (middle node):

$$
N_2(x) = \frac{(x - x_1)(x - x_3)}{(x_2 - x_1)(x_2 - x_3)} = \frac{(x - 0)(x - L)}{(L/2)(-L/2)} = -\frac{4x(x-L)}{L^2}
$$

$$
N_2(x) = -\frac{4x^2}{L^2} + \frac{4x}{L}
$$

For $N_3$ (end node):

$$
N_3(x) = \frac{(x - x_1)(x - x_2)}{(x_3 - x_1)(x_3 - x_2)} = \frac{x(x - L/2)}{L \cdot (L/2)} = \frac{2x(x - L/2)}{L^2}
$$

$$
N_3(x) = \frac{2x^2}{L^2} - \frac{x}{L}
$$

### 3.2.4 Verification (Partition of Unity)

$$
N_1 + N_2 + N_3 = \left(\frac{2x^2}{L^2} - \frac{3x}{L} + 1\right) + \left(-\frac{4x^2}{L^2} + \frac{4x}{L}\right) + \left(\frac{2x^2}{L^2} - \frac{x}{L}\right)
$$

$$
= \left(\frac{2 - 4 + 2}{L^2}\right)x^2 + \left(\frac{-3 + 4 - 1}{L}\right)x + 1 = 0 + 0 + 1 = 1 \ \checkmark
$$

### 3.2.5 Transform to Natural Coordinates $\xi \in [-1, +1]$

Using $x = \tfrac{L}{2}(1+\xi)$, after algebraic simplification:

$$
N_1(\xi) = \frac{-\xi(1-\xi)}{2}, \qquad N_2(\xi) = (1-\xi)(1+\xi) = 1 - \xi^2, \qquad N_3(\xi) = \frac{\xi(1+\xi)}{2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Note:** The quadratic 1-D element produces **linear strain** within the element, which is one degree higher than the constant strain of the linear element. This is precisely why quadratic elements are preferred for beam bending analysis.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: CST Shape Functions in Area Coordinates

### 3.3.1 Setup

A CST element has 3 corner nodes $i, j, k$ located at $(x_i, y_i)$, $(x_j, y_j)$, $(x_k, y_k)$.

### 3.3.2 Linear Displacement Field

$$
u(x,y) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 y
$$

$$
v(x,y) = b_0 + b_1 x + b_2 y
$$

### 3.3.3 Apply Boundary Conditions

Solving gives:

$$
u = N_i u_i + N_j u_j + N_k u_k, \qquad v = N_i v_i + N_j v_j + N_k v_k
$$

where:

$$
N_i(x,y) = \frac{a_i + b_i x + c_i y}{2A}, \quad N_j(x,y) = \frac{a_j + b_j x + c_j y}{2A}, \quad N_k(x,y) = \frac{a_k + b_k x + c_k y}{2A}
$$

and the geometric coefficients are:

$$
a_i = x_j y_k - x_k y_j, \quad b_i = y_j - y_k, \quad c_i = x_k - x_j
$$

$$
2A = (x_j - x_i)(y_k - y_i) - (x_k - x_i)(y_j - y_i)
$$

where $A$ is the area of the triangle.

### 3.3.4 Area-Coordinate Form

Define **area coordinates** (also called *barycentric* or *triangular* coordinates):

$$
L_i = \frac{A_i}{A}, \quad L_j = \frac{A_j}{A}, \quad L_k = \frac{A_k}{A}
$$

where $A_i$ is the area of the sub-triangle formed by the interior point and the side opposite to node $i$.

The shape functions become remarkably simple:

$$
N_i = L_i, \qquad N_j = L_j, \qquad N_k = L_k
$$

With the constraint:

$$
L_i + L_j + L_k = 1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Interpretation of $L_i$:** $L_i$ equals the ratio of the sub-triangle area $A_i$ to the total element area $A$. At node $i$, $L_i = 1$; on the edge opposite to node $i$, $L_i = 0$. This makes $L_i$ act as a "switch" that smoothly fades from 1 at the node to 0 at the opposite edge — exactly what a shape function must do.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this matrix in KTU answers to demonstrate comprehensive knowledge.**

| Element Type | Geometry | Nodes | Total DOF (2D Plane Stress) | Polynomial Order | Strain Variation | Accuracy | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bar (1-D linear) | Line segment | 2 | 4 | 1 | Constant | Low | Very Low |
| Bar (1-D quadratic) | Line segment | 3 | 6 | 2 | Linear | Medium | Low |
| Beam (Euler-Bernoulli) | Line segment | 2 | 6 | 3 (Hermite) | — | High (bending) | Medium |
| CST | Triangle | 3 | 6 | 1 | Constant | Low | Very Low |
| LST | Triangle | 6 | 12 | 2 | Linear | High | Medium |
| Q4 | Quadrilateral | 4 | 8 | 1 (bilinear) | Bilinear | Medium | Low |
| Q8 | Quadrilateral | 8 | 16 | 2 (serendipity) | Quadratic | High | Medium |
| Q9 | Quadrilateral | 9 | 18 | 2 (Lagrangian) | Quadratic | Very High | High |
| Tet4 (linear) | Tetrahedron | 4 | 12 | 1 | Constant | Low | Medium |
| Tet10 (quadratic) | Tetrahedron | 10 | 30 | 2 | Linear | High | High |
| Hex8 (linear brick) | Hexahedron | 8 | 24 | 1 (trilinear) | Trilinear | Medium | Medium |
| Hex20 (serendipity brick) | Hexahedron | 20 | 60 | 2 | Quadratic | Very High | High |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define a finite element. List any four types of 1-D finite elements based on their polynomial order.**

**Model Answer:**
A *finite element* is a small sub-region of a continuous domain over which the unknown field variable (displacement, temperature, etc.) is approximated by simple interpolation polynomials using the nodal values as coefficients.

Four types of 1-D elements based on polynomial order:
1. **Linear 1-D element** — 2 nodes, polynomial order 1 (e.g., 2-node truss/bar).
2. **Quadratic 1-D element** — 3 nodes, polynomial order 2.
3. **Cubic 1-D element** — 4 nodes, polynomial order 3.
4. **Quartic 1-D element** — 5 nodes, polynomial order 4.

*(Any 4 valid types accepted. Award full 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**What is an isoparametric element? Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**
An **isoparametric element** is one in which the **same set of shape functions** is used to interpolate both the **geometry** (coordinates) and the **field variable** (e.g., displacement) within the element. The word *iso* means "equal," referring to the equal polynomial order used for both mappings.

**Examples:**
1. **2-node linear bar/truss element** — uses linear shape functions $N_1 = \tfrac{1-\xi}{2}$, $N_2 = \tfrac{1+\xi}{2}$ for both $x$ and $u$.
2. **4-node bilinear quadrilateral (Q4)** — uses bilinear shape functions for both $(x, y)$ and $(u, v)$.
3. *(Acceptable: 8-node serendipity quad, 20-node brick, etc.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a)** Derive the shape functions of a **two-node linear 1-D element** in terms of the natural coordinate $\xi$. Show all steps. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain with neat sketches the **CST (Constant Strain Triangle)** and **LST (Linear Strain Triangle)** elements. Compare them in terms of nodes, DOF, polynomial order, strain variation, and accuracy. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Define geometry.** A 1-D linear element has two end nodes at physical coordinates $x_1$ and $x_2$ with corresponding nodal displacements $u_1$ and $u_2$.

**Step 2 — Choose interpolation polynomial.**

$$
u(x) = a_0 + a_1 x
$$

**Step 3 — Apply nodal boundary conditions.**

$$
u(x_1) = u_1 = a_0 + a_1 x_1
$$

$$
u(x_2) = u_2 = a_0 + a_1 x_2
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $a_0, a_1$.**

$$
a_0 = \frac{u_1 x_2 - u_2 x_1}{x_2 - x_1}, \qquad a_1 = \frac{u_2 - u_1}{x_2 - x_1}
$$

**Step 5 — Express $u(x)$ in terms of nodal displacements.**

$$
u(x) = \frac{x_2 - x}{x_2 - x_1}\, u_1 + \frac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1}\, u_2
$$

**Step 6 — Identify shape functions.**

$$
N_1(x) = \frac{x_2 - x}{x_2 - x_1}, \qquad N_2(x) = \frac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1}
$$

**Step 7 — Map to natural coordinate $\xi \in [-1, +1]$.** Using $x = \tfrac{x_1 + x_2}{2} + \tfrac{x_2 - x_1}{2}\,\xi$ and $L = x_2 - x_1$:

$$
N_1(\xi) = \frac{1 - \xi}{2}, \qquad N_2(\xi) = \frac{1 + \xi}{2}
$$

**Verification:**
- At $\xi = -1$: $N_1 = 1, N_2 = 0$ → $u = u_1$ ✓
- At $\xi = +1$: $N_1 = 0, N_2 = 1$ → $u = u_2$ ✓
- Partition of unity: $N_1 + N_2 = 1$ ✓

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**CST (Constant Strain Triangle):**

- **Geometry:** Triangle with **3 corner nodes** at $(x_i, y_i), (x_j, y_j), (x_k, y_k)$.
- **DOF:** 6 (2 per node: $u, v$).
- **Shape functions:** $N_i = L_i, N_j = L_j, N_k = L_k$ where $L$ are area coordinates with $L_i + L_j + L_k = 1$.
- **Polynomial order:** Linear (1st degree).
- **Strain:** $\varepsilon_x, \varepsilon_y, \gamma_{xy}$ are all **constant** throughout the element → hence the name "constant strain."
- **Accuracy:** Lowest among triangular elements; tends to be overly stiff.
- **Use case:** Initial mesh, complex curved boundaries, transition zones.

**LST (Linear Strain Triangle):**

- **Geometry:** Triangle with **6 nodes** — 3 corner nodes + 3 mid-side nodes.
- **DOF:** 12 (2 per node).
- **Shape functions:** Quadratic in $x, y$ (complete quadratic polynomial with 6 terms).
- **Polynomial order:** Quadratic (2nd degree).
- **Strain:** Strains vary **linearly** within the element → hence "linear strain."
- **Accuracy:** Significantly higher than CST; better representation of stress gradients.
- **Use case:** Regions with high stress gradients (stress concentrations, notches).

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | CST | LST |
|---|---|---|
| Nodes | 3 | 6 |
| DOF (plane stress) | 6 | 12 |
| Polynomial order | 1 (linear) | 2 (quadratic) |
| Strain variation | Constant | Linear |
| Shape function form | $L_i$ (area coordinates) | Quadratic in $L$ |
| Accuracy | Low | High |
| Computational cost | Low | ~4× higher |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **-2 marks** if you confuse the *number of nodes* with the *polynomial order*. CST = 3 nodes but Order 1. LST = 6 nodes but Order 2.
> - **-1 mark** if you forget to mention the **strain variation** property, which is the key difference.
> - **-1 mark** if you don't define area coordinates $L_i + L_j + L_k = 1$ in your answer.
> - **Diagrams are mandatory** for full marks on this question. Sketches without proper node numbering will cost you at least 2 marks.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain **isoparametric, sub-parametric, and super-parametric elements**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Derive the **shape functions of a 3-node quadratic 1-D bar element** in natural coordinates $\xi \in [-1, +1]$. Show that they satisfy the partition-of-unity property. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Isoparametric Element:**

- *Definition:* The **same shape functions** $N_i$ are used for mapping **both geometry and displacement/field variable**.
- *Examples:* 2-node bar (linear-linear), 3-node bar (quadratic-quadratic), Q4 quadrilateral, 8-node serendipity quad, 20-node brick.
- *Schematic:*

```
   Physical Element (x)       Natural Element (ξ)
   1---------2                -1=========+1
   Geometry:  Sum N_i(ξ) x_i
   Field:     Sum N_i(ξ) u_i     (same N_i)
```

**Sub-parametric Element:**

- *Definition:* The **displacement is interpolated with a higher polynomial order** than the geometry.
- *Example:* Geometry interpolated linearly (2 nodes) but displacement interpolated quadratically (3 nodes added as mid-side nodes).
- *Use:* Rare in practice; usually arises when geometry is simple but field solution needs refinement.

**Super-parametric Element:**

- *Definition:* The **geometry is interpolated with a higher polynomial order** than the displacement/field variable.
- *Example:* Geometry uses 8-node quadratic mapping while displacement uses 4-node bilinear mapping.
- *Use:* Modeling curved boundaries accurately while keeping the field approximation simple.

**Tabular Comparison:**

| Type | Geometry Order | Field Order | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Iso-parametric** | Same | Same | Most general-purpose work |
| **Sub-parametric** | Lower | Higher | Field refinement on simple geometry |
| **Super-parametric** | Higher | Lower | Curved boundaries, simple fields |

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Geometry and node placement.** A 3-node 1-D element has nodes at:
- Node 1: $x_1 = 0$, displacement $u_1$.
- Node 2: $x_2 = L/2$, displacement $u_2$.
- Node 3: $x_3 = L$, displacement $u_3$.

**Step 2 — Choose quadratic polynomial.**

$$
u(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2
$$

**Step 3 — Apply nodal conditions:**

$$
u_1 = a_0
$$

$$
u_2 = a_0 + a_1 \frac{L}{2} + a_2 \frac{L^2}{4}
$$

$$
u_3 = a_0 + a_1 L + a_2 L^2
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Lagrange interpolation formula.** For node $i$, the Lagrange shape function is:

$$
N_i(x) = \prod_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{3} \frac{x - x_j}{x_i - x_j}
$$

**For $N_1$:**

$$
N_1(x) = \frac{(x - L/2)(x - L)}{(0 - L/2)(0 - L)} = \frac{2(x - L/2)(x - L)}{L^2}
$$

Expanding:

$$
N_1(x) = \frac{2x^2}{L^2} - \frac{3x}{L} + 1
$$

**For $N_2$ (mid-node):**

$$
N_2(x) = \frac{x(x - L)}{(L/2)(-L/2)} = -\frac{4x(x - L)}{L^2} = -\frac{4x^2}{L^2} + \frac{4x}{L}
$$

**For $N_3$:**

$$
N_3(x) = \frac{x(x - L/2)}{L \cdot (L/2)} = \frac{2x(x - L/2)}{L^2} = \frac{2x^2}{L^2} - \frac{x}{L}
$$

**Step 5 — Transform to natural coordinates $\xi \in [-1, +1]$ using $x = \tfrac{L}{2}(1 + \xi)$:**

$$
N_1(\xi) = \frac{-\xi(1 - \xi)}{2}, \qquad N_2(\xi) = (1 - \xi)(1 + \xi) = 1 - \xi^2, \qquad N_3(\xi) = \frac{\xi(1 + \xi)}{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify partition of unity:**

$$
N_1 + N_2 + N_3 = \frac{-\xi(1-\xi)}{2} + (1 - \xi^2) + \frac{\xi(1+\xi)}{2}
$$

$$
= \frac{-\xi + \xi^2 + 2 - 2\xi^2 + \xi + \xi^2}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1 \ \checkmark
$$

**Verification at nodes:**
- $\xi = -1$: $N_1 = 1, N_2 = 0, N_3 = 0$ ✓
- $\xi = 0$: $N_1 = 0, N_2 = 1, N_3 = 0$ ✓
- $\xi = +1$: $N_1 = 0, N_2 = 0, N_3 = 1$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls for Q.B:**
> - **-2 marks** if you forget the partition-of-unity verification step explicitly — it is *required* by the question.
> - **-1 mark** if you do not explicitly evaluate each $N_i$ at the three natural coordinate values ($\xi = -1, 0, +1$) to show the Kronecker delta property.
> - **-2 marks** if you skip the transformation to natural coordinates. KTU examiners specifically test whether students can switch between physical and natural coordinates fluently.
> - **-1 mark** if you confuse $L$ (element length) with the Lagrange function notation. Use a clearly different symbol like $\ell$ if needed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Types of Elements (Module 1)**

- ✅ **Definition:** A finite element is a sub-region of the domain with polynomial-interpolated field variables; "type" refers to its dimensionality, geometry, polynomial order, and parameterization.
- ✅ **4 Classification Axes:** Dimensionality (1D/2D/3D) × Geometry × Polynomial Order × Parameterization (Iso/Sub/Super).
- ✅ **Linear 1-D shape functions:** $N_1 = \tfrac{1-\xi}{2}, \ N_2 = \tfrac{1+\xi}{2}$.
- ✅ **Quadratic 1-D shape functions:** $N_1 = \tfrac{-\xi(1-\xi)}{2}, \ N_2 = 1-\xi^2, \ N_3 = \tfrac{\xi(1+\xi)}{2}$.
- ✅ **CST:** 3-node triangle, **constant strain**, linear shape functions in area coordinates.
- ✅ **LST:** 6-node triangle, **linear strain**, quadratic shape functions.
- ✅ **Q4:** 4-node quadrilateral, **bilinear** shape functions, $8 \times 8$ stiffness matrix.
- ✅ **Q8:** 8-node quadrilateral, **quadratic serendipity**, $16 \times 16$ stiffness matrix.
- ✅ **20-node brick:** Workhorse of 3-D FEM, $60 \times 60$ stiffness matrix.
- ✅ **Isoparametric** = same $N_i$ for geometry and field (most common in modern FEM codes).
- ✅ **Total DOF per element:** $n \times d$ where $n$ = nodes, $d$ = DOF per node.
- ✅ **Partition of unity:** Always $\sum N_i = 1$ — verify this in every derivation.
- ✅ **Kronecker delta property:** $N_i(x_j) = \delta_{ij}$ — shape functions equal 1 at "own" node and 0 at all other nodes.
- ✅ **Natural coordinate range:** Always $\xi \in [-1, +1]$ for the parent (master) element in isoparametric formulations.
- ✅ **Polynomial order vs number of nodes:** Don't confuse them. Quadratic 1-D = 3 nodes but Order 2.
- ✅ **Higher-order ≠ always better:** They have more DOF per element → larger matrices → higher cost per element, though fewer elements may be needed overall.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formulation of Stiffness Matrix - One Dimensional Spring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET634 - FINITE ELEMENT METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Formulation of Stiffness Matrix - One Dimensional Spring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition: Element Stiffness Matrix (Spring Element)**
> For a linear one-dimensional spring element of stiffness **k** connecting two nodes (local nodes **1** and **2**), the **Element Stiffness Matrix** **[k<sup>(e)</sup>]** is a symmetric 2×2 matrix that linearly relates the nodal force vector **{F}<sup>(e)</sup>** to the nodal displacement vector **{d}<sup>(e)</sup>** through the elemental equilibrium equation:
>
> **{F}<sup>(e)</sup> = [k<sup>(e)</sup>] {d}<sup>(e)</sup>**

For a linear spring, the element stiffness matrix has the canonical form:

$$[k^{(e)}] = k \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where the diagonal terms are positive (representing the restoring force at a node due to its own displacement) and the off-diagonal terms are negative (representing the influence of the adjacent node).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Engineering Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Coupled Spring-Pulley System**
> Imagine two trolleys of mass **m** each connected by a single spring on a frictionless horizontal track. The trolleys can move horizontally, and the spring can only stretch or compress along its axis. If you push the left trolley to the right, the spring compresses and pushes the right trolley forward. If you pull the left trolley to the left, the spring stretches and drags the right trolley backward. The spring's **stiffness k** (in N/m) determines how strongly it resists deformation.

In this analogy:
- **Trolley positions** ↔ **Nodal displacements** (u₁, u₂)
- **Forces you apply on trolleys** ↔ **External nodal forces** (F₁, F₂)
- **Spring's internal restoring force** ↔ **Internal element force**
- **The 2×2 stiffness matrix** ↔ A compact mathematical "translator" that converts nodal displacements into the equivalent nodal forces

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Interpretation of the Matrix Terms**
> - **k₁₁ = +k** : Force at node 1 due to a unit displacement at node 1 (self-stiffness — always positive).
> - **k₁₂ = −k** : Force at node 1 due to a unit displacement at node 2 (coupling stiffness — always negative for a tension/compression spring).
> - **k₂₁ = −k** : Force at node 2 due to a unit displacement at node 1 (by symmetry of stiffness, this equals k₁₂).
> - **k₂₂ = +k** : Force at node 2 due to a unit displacement at node 2 (self-stiffness — always positive).

## 1.3 Key Engineering Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit (SI) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **k** | Spring stiffness / rigidity | **N/m** (Newton per metre) |
| **u** | Nodal displacement | **m** (metre) |
| **F** | Nodal force | **N** (Newton) |
| **L** | Length of spring | **m** (metre) |
| **δ** | Elongation / deformation of spring | **m** (metre) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Energy Method vs. Direct Stiffness Method

The element stiffness matrix for a 1-D spring can be derived using **two equivalent approaches**, both of which are board-favorite questions in KTU ESE:

### Approach 1: Direct Equilibrium Method (Newtonian Approach)
> [!NOTE]
> Hooke's Law for the spring: The internal force in the spring is **F = k(u₂ − u₁)**. Applying equilibrium at the two free ends of the spring gives the two equations of the matrix form.

- If node 1 is displaced by u₁ and node 2 by u₂, the elongation of the spring is **δ = u₂ − u₁**.
- Internal spring force (tension positive): **F_int = k(u₂ − u₁)**
- At node 1, the external force F₁ must balance the spring force: **F₁ = −F_int = −k(u₂ − u₁) = k·u₁ − k·u₂**
- At node 2, the external force F₂ must balance: **F₂ = +F_int = k(u₂ − u₁) = −k·u₁ + k·u₂**

Writing these two equations in matrix form yields the canonical stiffness matrix.

### Approach 2: Variational / Energy Method (Principle of Minimum Potential Energy)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests the **Rayleigh-Ritz / Potential Energy Approach** because it extends naturally to bars, trusses, beams, and 2D/3D elements. Mastering the energy method is the single most important step to score well in FEM.

- Total Potential Energy Π = Strain Energy (U) − Work done by external forces (W)
- For a linear spring: **U = ½ k (u₂ − u₁)²**
- Work done: **W = F₁u₁ + F₂u₂**
- Setting **∂Π/∂u₁ = 0** and **∂Π/∂u₂ = 0** gives the equilibrium equations.

## 2.2 The Three Phases of FEM Analysis (For Spring Systems)

1. **Pre-Processing Phase (Discretization)**
   - Divide the spring/structural system into discrete elements.
   - Number all nodes globally (1, 2, 3, ...) and all elements locally.
   - Define the connectivity / incidence table (which nodes belong to which element).
   - Identify boundary conditions (prescribed displacements = essential/kinematic BCs).
   - Identify external loads (prescribed forces = natural/force BCs).

2. **Processing Phase (Element & Global Analysis)**
   - Compute the **element stiffness matrix [k<sup>(e)</sup>]= k[[1, −1], [−1, 1]]** for each element.
   - Expand each element matrix to global size (size = total DOF = total free nodes for 1-D).
   - **Assemble** the Global Stiffness Matrix **[K]** by direct stiffness method (superposition of element contributions at shared nodes).
   - Apply boundary conditions (modify [K] and {F}).
   - Solve the reduced system **[K]{U} = {F}** for unknown nodal displacements.

3. **Post-Processing Phase (Recovery)**
   - Recover element forces / stresses from nodal displacements: **F<sup>(e)</sup> = k(u₂ − u₁)**.
   - Compute reactions at supports (by back-substitution into rows of the original global equilibrium).

## 2.3 Properties of the Element Stiffness Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

> [!TIP]
> These properties are exam-favourite 2-mark and 3-mark questions. Memorize them!

- **Symmetry**: [k] = [k]<sup>T</sup> — a direct consequence of Maxwell–Betti's reciprocal work theorem.
- **Singularity**: The element matrix is **singular** (determinant = 0) on its own because rigid-body motion is not restrained — it must be supported by neighbouring elements or boundary conditions in the global system.
- **Order of matrix**: For a 1-D spring with 2 nodes (each having 1 translational DOF), the order is 2×2.
- **Diagonal dominance**: The diagonal term equals the sum of absolute off-diagonal terms in its row (or column). This makes the matrix physically stable.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Application / Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $F = k \cdot \delta = k(u_2 - u_1)$ | Hooke's Law for spring | Force in spring given nodal displacements |
| 2 | $\begin{bmatrix} F_1 \\ F_2 \end{bmatrix} = k \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix}$ | Element stiffness equation | Relates nodal forces to nodal displacements |
| 3 | $[k^{(e)}] = k \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Element stiffness matrix (2×2) | Core FEM building block |
| 4 | $\Pi = \tfrac{1}{2} k (u_2 - u_1)^2 - F_1 u_1 - F_2 u_2$ | Total Potential Energy | Energy method derivation |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\partial \Pi}{\partial u_1} = 0, \ \dfrac{\partial \Pi}{\partial u_2} = 0$ | Stationarity of Π | Derives the equilibrium equations |
| 6 | $[K] \{U\} = \{F\}$ | Global equilibrium equation | Solves for unknown nodal displacements |
| 7 | $\sigma^{(e)} = \dfrac{F^{(e)}}{A}$ | Stress in axial members | Used in bar/truss extensions |
| 8 | $\sum F_x = 0$ at every node | Node equilibrium check | Validates assembled system |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The 1-D spring stiffness formulation is the **conceptual foundation** for:
> - **Truss and frame analysis** (axial members behave like springs)
> - **Bar elements with stiffness AE/L** (axial stiffness of a bar)
> - **Spring-mass vibration systems** (modal and frequency analysis)
> - **Boundary element & substructure coupling**
> - **Soil-structure interaction modeling** (Winkler foundation = series of independent springs)
> - **Micro-electromechanical systems (MEMS)** and biomechanics

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Element Stiffness Matrix from Direct Equilibrium

### Step 1: Define the Spring Element
Consider a single linear spring of stiffness **k** (N/m) connected between two nodes, **node 1** (left) and **node 2** (right). Each node has **one translational degree of freedom** (DOF) along the spring axis. Let:
- $u_1$ = displacement of node 1
- $u_2$ = displacement of node 2
- $F_1$ = external force applied at node 1
- $F_2$ = external force applied at node 2

### Step 2: Write the Hooke's Law Expression
The elongation (or compression) of the spring is the relative displacement of its two ends:

$$\delta = u_2 - u_1$$

By Hooke's Law, the internal tensile force in the spring is:

$$F_{int} = k \cdot \delta = k(u_2 - u_1)$$

### Step 3: Apply Node Equilibrium
At **node 1**: The external force F₁ must balance the pull of the spring on node 1. Since the spring pulls node 1 to the right when stretched, the equilibrium gives:

$$F_1 = -F_{int} = -k(u_2 - u_1)$$

Expanding:

$$F_1 = -k \cdot u_2 + k \cdot u_1$$

Rearranging in the form **{F} = [k] {u}**:

$$F_1 = k \cdot u_1 - k \cdot u_2$$

At **node 2**: The external force F₂ must balance the pull of the spring on node 2:

$$F_2 = +F_{int} = k(u_2 - u_1)$$

Expanding:

$$F_2 = -k \cdot u_1 + k \cdot u_2$$

### Step 4: Write in Matrix Form
Stacking the two equations:

$$\begin{Bmatrix} F_1 \\ F_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} k & -k \\ -k & k \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

Factoring out k:

$$\begin{Bmatrix} F_1 \\ F_2 \end{Bmatrix} = k \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

### Step 5: Identify the Element Stiffness Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result — Element Stiffness Matrix of a 1-D Linear Spring:**
>
> $$\boxed{[k^{(e)}] = k \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}$$

This is the **2×2 element stiffness matrix** in its canonical form. It is symmetric (k₁₂ = k₂₁ = −k), positive semi-definite, and singular in isolation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Element Stiffness Matrix from the Principle of Minimum Potential Energy

This is the **rigorous variational method** — the same method that extends to bars, beams, and 2D/3D continua. KTU may ask the derivation using this method for 7 marks.

### Step 1: Express the Strain Energy U
For a linear spring, the strain energy is half the product of force and elongation:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} F_{int} \cdot \delta = \frac{1}{2} k \delta^2$$

Substituting δ = u₂ − u₁:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} k (u_2 - u_1)^2$$

### Step 2: Express the Work Done by External Forces
The applied nodal forces F₁ and F₂ perform work as their respective nodes move:

$$W = F_1 u_1 + F_2 u_2$$

### Step 3: Form the Total Potential Energy
By definition, the total potential energy of the system is:

$$\Pi = U - W$$

Substituting:

$$\Pi = \frac{1}{2} k (u_2 - u_1)^2 - F_1 u_1 - F_2 u_2$$

### Step 4: Expand the Strain Energy Term
We expand (u₂ − u₁)² = u₁² − 2u₁u₂ + u₂²:

$$\Pi = \frac{1}{2} k (u_1^2 - 2 u_1 u_2 + u_2^2) - F_1 u_1 - F_2 u_2$$

### Step 5: Differentiate Π with Respect to u₁
Treating u₂ as constant and using ∂/∂u₁:

$$\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial u_1} = \frac{1}{2} k (2 u_1 - 2 u_2) - F_1 = k(u_1 - u_2) - F_1$$

Setting this equal to zero for stationary potential energy:

$$\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial u_1} = 0 \implies k u_1 - k u_2 = F_1$$

### Step 6: Differentiate Π with Respect to u₂
Treating u₁ as constant:

$$\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial u_2} = \frac{1}{2} k (-2 u_1 + 2 u_2) - F_2 = -k u_1 + k u_2 - F_2$$

Setting this equal to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial u_2} = 0 \implies -k u_1 + k u_2 = F_2$$

### Step 7: Write the Two Equations in Matrix Form
Stacking the two results:

$$\begin{bmatrix} k & -k \\ -k & k \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} F_1 \\ F_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

Factoring out the scalar k:

$$k \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} F_1 \\ F_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

### Step 8: Identify the Element Stiffness Matrix (Same Result)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result confirmed via variational method:**
>
> $$\boxed{[k^{(e)}] = k \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}$$
>
> This is **identical** to the result from the direct equilibrium method — confirming the consistency of the FEM formulation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Framework: Assembly of the Global Stiffness Matrix

For a system with **n** spring elements and **N** nodes (where N = n + 1 for a chain of springs in series), the global stiffness matrix **[K]** of size N×N is assembled by **superposing the element contributions** at the shared nodes.

### Worked Example: Two Springs in Series

**Problem:** Two springs of stiffness k₁ and k₂ are connected in series between nodes 1, 2, and 3, with node 1 fixed and a force P applied at node 3. Find the global stiffness matrix, the displacement at node 2 and node 3, and the reactions.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Element Matrices**
- Element 1 (between nodes 1 and 2):
$$[k^{(1)}] = k_1 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
- Element 2 (between nodes 2 and 3):
$$[k^{(2)}] = k_2 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Expand to Global Size (3×3)**
Element 1 (nodes 1, 2) expanded:
$$[k^{(1)}]_{global} = k_1 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
Element 2 (nodes 2, 3) expanded:
$$[k^{(2)}]_{global} = k_2 \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Assemble by Superposition**
Adding element matrices element-by-element:

$$[K] = k_1 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} + k_2 \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$[K] = \begin{bmatrix} k_1 & -k_1 & 0 \\ -k_1 & k_1 + k_2 & -k_2 \\ 0 & -k_2 & k_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Global Equilibrium Equation**
With the force vector {F} = {0, 0, P}<sup>T</sup> (no force at fixed node 1 or free node 2):

$$\begin{bmatrix} k_1 & -k_1 & 0 \\ -k_1 & k_1 + k_2 & -k_2 \\ 0 & -k_2 & k_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \\ u_3 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ P \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Apply Boundary Condition**
u₁ = 0 (fixed support) → eliminate first row and column:

$$\begin{bmatrix} k_1 + k_2 & -k_2 \\ -k_2 & k_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_2 \\ u_3 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ P \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step 6: Solve the Reduced System**
From the first equation: (k₁ + k₂)u₂ − k₂u₃ = 0 ⟹ u₂ = (k₂/(k₁ + k₂)) u₃
Substituting into the second: −k₂u₂ + k₂u₃ = P

$$-\frac{k_2^2}{k_1 + k_2} u_3 + k_2 u_3 = P$$

$$k_2 \left( 1 - \frac{k_2}{k_1 + k_2} \right) u_3 = P$$

$$k_2 \cdot \frac{k_1}{k_1 + k_2} u_3 = P$$

$$\boxed{u_3 = \frac{P(k_1 + k_2)}{k_1 k_2} = P \left( \frac{1}{k_1} + \frac{1}{k_2} \right)}$$

This result is the famous **series-spring formula** in disguise — verifying the correctness of FEM.

And:

$$\boxed{u_2 = \frac{k_2}{k_1 + k_2} \cdot u_3 = \frac{P}{k_1}}$$

**Step 7: Compute the Reaction at Node 1**
Using the first row of the original (unreduced) global equation:

$$R_1 = k_1 u_1 - k_1 u_2 = 0 - k_1 \cdot \frac{P}{k_1} = -P$$

The negative sign indicates that the reaction acts opposite to the assumed positive direction — equilibrium is satisfied because the applied force P and reaction R₁ are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of FEM Spring Analysis

The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the complete workflow of FEM analysis of a 1-D spring system, from physical problem to post-processed results.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PHYSICAL PROBLEM: 1D Spring System with N nodes and M elements"] --> B["STEP 1: DISCRETIZATION - Identify nodes, elements, DOFs"]
    B --> C["STEP 2: CONNECTIVITY - Build incidence table: element-to-node mapping"]
    C --> D["STEP 3: ELEMENT STIFFNESS - Compute k_e matrix for each spring element"]
    D --> E["STEP 4: GLOBAL EXPANSION - Embed each k_e into global K matrix of size N by N"]
    E --> F["STEP 5: ASSEMBLY - Sum element contributions at shared nodes"]
    F --> G["STEP 6: APPLY BOUNDARY CONDITIONS - Essential BC: u_known; Natural BC: F_known"]
    G --> H["STEP 7: PARTITION SYSTEM - Separate known displacements from unknown ones"]
    H --> I["STEP 8: SOLVE Ku equals F - Compute unknown nodal displacements"]
    I --> J["STEP 9: BACK SUBSTITUTION - Compute reactions at fixed supports"]
    J --> K["STEP 10: POST PROCESS - Compute element internal forces and stresses"]
    K --> L["FINAL OUTPUT: Nodal displacements, reactions, element forces"]

```

## 4.2 Schematic of the Spring Element with Nodal Conventions

The following **Mermaid diagram** shows the standard convention for a 1-D spring element — nodal forces, displacements, and the sign convention used in deriving the stiffness matrix.

    A["NODE 1: Force F1 to the right, displacement u1"] -- "Spring of stiffness k" --> B["NODE 2: Force F2 to the right, displacement u2"]

- **Red-filled nodes** denote **fixed (constrained) supports** with known displacement.
- **Green-filled nodes** denote **free nodes with zero applied force** (interior nodes).
- **Blue-filled nodes** denote **free nodes with non-zero applied force** (loaded nodes).
- Arrows labelled "Element n: stiffness kₙ" represent the spring elements in the discretized system.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Definition of Element Stiffness Matrix
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Q: Define the element stiffness matrix for a one-dimensional linear spring element. State its important properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The element stiffness matrix of a 1-D linear spring of stiffness **k** is a 2×2 matrix that relates the nodal force vector to the nodal displacement vector through the equilibrium equation **{F} = [k]{u}**. For a spring with two nodes (1 and 2) along its axis:

$$[k^{(e)}] = k \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Important properties** (2 of these required for full marks):
1. **Symmetric**: [k<sup>(e)</sup>] = [k<sup>(e)</sup>]<sup>T</sup> — consequence of Maxwell–Betti's reciprocal theorem.
2. **Singular in isolation**: det([k<sup>(e)</sup>]) = 0 — rigid-body modes are not restrained, so it must be supported in the assembled global system.
3. **Diagonally dominant**: The diagonal term equals the sum of absolute off-diagonal terms.
4. **Positive semi-definite**: All eigenvalues are ≥ 0.
5. **Order**: For a 1-D spring with 2 nodes × 1 DOF/node, the order is always 2×2.

---

### Question A2: Physical Interpretation
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Q: Explain the physical meaning of the terms k₁₁, k₁₂, k₂₁, and k₂₂ in the element stiffness matrix of a spring. Why is k₁₂ negative?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For a linear spring element stiffness matrix [k<sup>(e)</sup>] = k [[1, −1], [−1, 1]]:
- **k₁₁ = +k** : The force required at node 1 to produce a unit displacement at node 1, while keeping node 2 fixed. It represents the **self-stiffness** or **direct stiffness** of the spring at node 1.
- **k₂₂ = +k** : The force required at node 2 to produce a unit displacement at node 2, while keeping node 1 fixed. It is the self-stiffness at node 2.
- **k₁₂ = −k** : The force induced at node 1 when node 2 is displaced by a unit amount, with node 1 fixed. It is the **coupling stiffness** between the two nodes.
- **k₂₁ = −k** : The force induced at node 2 when node 1 is displaced by a unit amount. By **Maxwell–Betti's reciprocal theorem**, k₁₂ = k₂₁.

**Why is k₁₂ negative?** When node 2 is displaced positively (moved to the right) and node 1 is held fixed, the spring gets **compressed**. The compressed spring pushes node 1 to the **left**, which is in the **negative direction**. Hence the force at node 1 is negative, giving k₁₂ = −k.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> Following KTU ESE 2024 Scheme, each Part B question carries 14 marks split into two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice must be provided within the module.

---

### Question B1 (14 Marks) — *Option A*
> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**
> **(a)** Derive the element stiffness matrix for a one-dimensional linear spring element using the **principle of minimum potential energy**. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** For the spring system shown in the figure below, three springs of stiffness **k** are connected in series between a fixed wall (node 1) and a free end (node 4) where a horizontal force **P** is applied. Nodes 2 and 3 are interior nodes. Assemble the **global stiffness matrix** and determine the nodal displacements u₂, u₃, u₄ and the reaction R₁. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Derivation using Minimum Potential Energy (7 marks):**

Total Potential Energy: $\Pi = U - W$

Strain Energy: $U = \frac{1}{2} k (u_2 - u_1)^2$

External Work: $W = F_1 u_1 + F_2 u_2$

So: $\Pi = \frac{1}{2} k (u_2 - u_1)^2 - F_1 u_1 - F_2 u_2$

Expanding:

$$\Pi = \frac{1}{2} k (u_1^2 - 2 u_1 u_2 + u_2^2) - F_1 u_1 - F_2 u_2$$

Stationarity conditions:

$$\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial u_1} = k(u_1 - u_2) - F_1 = 0 \implies k u_1 - k u_2 = F_1$$

$$\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial u_2} = -k(u_1 - u_2) - F_2 = 0 \implies -k u_1 + k u_2 = F_2$$

In matrix form:

$$[k] = k \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(3 marks for derivation, 2 for final matrix, 2 for explanation)}$$

**Part (b) — Global Assembly and Solution (7 marks):**

**Element matrices** (each spring of stiffness k):

$$[k^{(1)}] = [k^{(2)}] = [k^{(3)}] = k \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Global assembly** (size 4×4, since 4 nodes):

$$[K] = k \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 & 0 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Boundary conditions**: u₁ = 0 (fixed), F₂ = F₃ = 0, F₄ = P.

**Reduced system** (rows 2, 3, 4):

$$k \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_2 \\ u_3 \\ u_4 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ P \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Solving by back-substitution or matrix inversion:**

From the third equation: $k(u_4 - u_3) = P \implies u_4 = u_3 + P/k$

From the second: $k(-u_2 + 2u_3 - u_4) = 0 \implies u_2 = 2u_3 - u_4 = u_3 - P/k$

From the first: $k(2u_2 - u_3) = 0 \implies 2(u_3 - P/k) = u_3 \implies u_3 = 2P/k$

Therefore: $u_3 = 2P/k$, $u_2 = P/k$, $u_4 = 3P/k$

**Reaction at node 1** (using the first row of full system):

$$R_1 = k(u_1 - u_2) = -k u_2 = -P$$

The reaction is −P (acts in negative direction to balance P).

---

### Question B2 (14 Marks) — *Option B (Internal Choice)*
> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **three basic steps** of the Finite Element Method (FEM) as applied to a one-dimensional spring system. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Two springs of stiffness **k₁ = 200 N/m** and **k₂ = 300 N/m** are connected in **parallel** between two rigid plates. A force of **P = 100 N** is applied. Determine the common displacement of the plates using the stiffness matrix method. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Three Basic Steps of FEM (7 marks):**

1. **Pre-Processing (Discretization)** (2 marks): The continuous spring system is divided into a finite number of spring elements connected at nodes. Number the nodes globally (1, 2, ..., N) and elements locally. Identify the connectivity table, material properties (k values), boundary conditions, and applied loads.

2. **Processing (Element Analysis & Assembly)** (3 marks):
   - For each spring element, compute the element stiffness matrix:
   $$[k^{(e)}] = k \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
   - Expand each element matrix to global size and assemble into the global stiffness matrix [K] by superposing element contributions at shared nodes.
   - Apply boundary conditions and solve [K]{U} = {F} for unknown nodal displacements.

3. **Post-Processing (Recovery of Results)** (2 marks): Compute the internal force in each spring element using $F^{(e)} = k(u_2 - u_1)$ and determine the support reactions by back-substitution into the original (unreduced) equilibrium equations.

**Part (b) — Parallel Spring System (7 marks):**

In a parallel configuration, **both springs have the same displacement** δ (since they connect the same two rigid plates).

**Element matrices:**
$$[k^{(1)}] = 200 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad [k^{(2)}] = 300 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Global stiffness matrix** (size 2×2 for 2 nodes):

$$[K] = (k_1 + k_2) \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = 500 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N/m}$$

**Apply boundary condition**: Node 1 is fixed, so u₁ = 0.

**Reduced system** (second row only):

$$500 \cdot (u_2 - 0) = 100 \implies \boxed{u_2 = 0.2 \text{ m}}$$

**Verification using classical formula** for parallel springs:

$$\delta = \frac{P}{k_1 + k_2} = \frac{100}{500} = 0.2 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms that the FEM stiffness matrix approach gives the same result as the classical mechanics formula — a powerful cross-verification technique.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors** (–2 to –3 marks): Students often write the stiffness matrix as `[[k, k], [k, k]]` instead of `[[k, –k], [–k, k]]`. Remember: the off-diagonals are **negative** because a positive displacement at one node produces a **negative reaction** at the other (compression of the spring).
> 2. **Confusing Element vs. Global Matrix** (–2 marks): The element matrix is **2×2** and the global matrix is **N×N** (where N is the number of nodes). Forgetting to expand the element matrix to global size before assembly is a frequent error.
> 3. **Forgetting Boundary Conditions** (–3 marks): Failing to apply the essential boundary condition (e.g., u₁ = 0 for a fixed support) before solving the system leads to a singular matrix and an undefined result.
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency** (–1 mark): Always use **SI units** — k in N/m, F in N, and u in m. Mixing units (e.g., kN with cm) is a guaranteed deduction.
> 5. **Skipping Derivations in 7-mark Questions** (–2 marks): For 7-mark sub-parts, the examiner expects **at least 5–6 clearly labeled derivation steps**. Do not write only the final matrix.
> 6. **Incorrect Assembly by Direct Summation** (–2 marks): When assembling, students sometimes add matrices **element by element incorrectly**. Always verify that the global K is symmetric and that the diagonal entry at node i is the **sum of stiffnesses of all elements meeting at node i**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

### Core Concepts
- [x] The element stiffness matrix of a 1-D spring is always **2×2** with the canonical form $k[[1, -1], [-1, 1]]$.
- [x] The matrix is **symmetric** (k₁₂ = k₂₁), **singular in isolation** (det = 0), and **positive semi-definite**.
- [x] The diagonal terms are positive (self-stiffness) and the off-diagonal terms are negative (coupling stiffness).
- [x] Two equivalent derivations exist: **Direct Equilibrium** and **Principle of Minimum Potential Energy** — both give the same result.

### Numerical Procedure (Memorize the 5-Step Process)
1. **Discretize** the system into elements and nodes; build the connectivity table.
2. **Compute** $[k^{(e)}] = k[[1, -1], [-1, 1]]$ for each element.
3. **Assemble** the global stiffness matrix [K] by direct superposition of expanded element matrices.
4. **Apply boundary conditions**: u_known = prescribed; reduce the system to solve for unknown displacements.
5. **Recover** element internal forces ($F^{(e)} = k(u_2 - u_1)$) and reactions.

### Key Equations (Final Formula Bank)
- Element stiffness matrix: $[k^{(e)}] = k[[1, -1], [-1, 1]]$
- Global equilibrium: $[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$
- Internal element force: $F^{(e)} = k(u_2 - u_1)$
- Series combination (compliance additivity): $\frac{1}{k_{eq}} = \frac{1}{k_1} + \frac{1}{k_2}$
- Parallel combination (stiffness additivity): $k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2$

### Common Exam Traps to Avoid
- Off-diagonals must be **negative**, not positive.
- The element matrix is **singular on its own** — singularity is removed only when boundary conditions are applied at the global level.
- For a chain of **N nodes** and **N−1 elements**, the global K is **N×N**.
- Reaction calculation requires the **original (unreduced) system** — not the reduced one.

### Recommended Study Order
1. Master the 2×2 element stiffness matrix derivation (both methods).
2. Practice 2- and 3-spring problems with manual assembly.
3. Learn how to apply essential boundary conditions and reduce the system.
4. Practice computing reactions and internal forces.
5. Move on to bar elements ($k = AE/L$) — the spring concept extends directly.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bar Element Assembly and Solution Procedure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET634 - FINITE ELEMENT METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bar Element Assembly and Solution Procedure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Bar Element Assembly & Solution Procedure:**
> The **assembly process** in the Finite Element Method (FEM) is the systematic procedure by which individual **element stiffness matrices** (computed in the local/global coordinate system of each bar/truss element) are systematically combined into a single **Global Stiffness Matrix (GSM) [K]** of the entire discretized domain. The **solution procedure** then involves modifying this [K] matrix to enforce **essential boundary conditions** (prescribed displacements), solving the resulting linear algebraic system **[K]{u} = {F}** for the unknown nodal displacements, and finally back-calculating the **element strains and stresses** via post-processing.

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus, this topic falls under the cognitive levels **Apply (L3)** and **Analyze (L4)** of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy, as students must both construct the assembled matrix for a multi-element bar and analyze the resulting field variables.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Saturated Sponge Chain"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for a First-Time Learner:**
> Imagine you have a chain of **N interconnected spring-coils** representing the bar segments. Each coil (element) has its own **local stiffness** $k^{(e)}$ determined by its material (Young's modulus **E**), cross-sectional area (**A**), and length (**L**). The assembly procedure is essentially "stitching" these coils together at the **shared nodes (junctions)** so that when you pull one end of the chain, the internal force is transmitted continuously from one coil to the next. The Global Stiffness Matrix [K] represents the *collective stiffness of the entire chain*, while the solution procedure is the act of *measuring how much each junction stretches* when external forces are applied — all governed by **Hooke's Law** ($F = k\delta$) generalized into matrix form.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Units Used

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit | KTU Standard Notation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **E** | Young's Modulus | **N/m²** or **Pa** | Constant for each element |
| **A** | Cross-sectional area | **m²** | Constant for each element |
| **L** | Length of bar element | **m** | $L^{(e)}$ |
| **u** | Nodal displacement | **m** | Component of $\{u\}$ |
| **F** | Nodal force | **N** | Component of $\{F\}$ |
| **σ** | Axial stress | **N/m²** (Pa) | Computed post-solution |
| **ε** | Axial strain | Dimensionless | Computed post-solution |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Assembly & Solution

The KTU 2024 scheme evaluates students on the following **sequential steps** for any bar/truss problem:

### Step A — Local Element Stiffness Matrix Formulation
For a 2-node linear bar element with nodes $i$ and $j$, the element stiffness matrix in **global coordinates** (which coincides with local for a 1D axial bar) is:

$$\begin{aligned}
[k^{(e)}] = \frac{A^{(e)} E^{(e)}}{L^{(e)}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Why?** Because the bar's strain energy $U = \frac{1}{2} \int \sigma \varepsilon \, dV$ leads to a quadratic form whose Hessian matrix yields exactly this bilinear stiffness — and the symmetry reflects **Maxwell–Betti's reciprocal work theorem**.

### Step B — DOF Connectivity (Loc-to-Glob Mapping)
Each element's local DOFs must be mapped to the **global DOF numbering**. For element $e$ connecting global nodes $p$ and $q$:

$$\{u^{(e)}\} = \begin{Bmatrix} u_p \\ u_q \end{Bmatrix}$$

This mapping is **vital** for the assembly summation. KTU board examiners specifically look for this mapping in the manual calculation part.

### Step C — Global Assembly (Direct Stiffness Method)
The global stiffness matrix is built using the **superposition principle** — the contribution of each element stiffness is added to the appropriate global matrix entries:

$$\begin{aligned}
[K] = \sum_{e=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}^{(e)\top} [k^{(e)}] \mathcal{L}^{(e)}
\end{aligned}$$

where $\mathcal{L}^{(e)}$ is the **Boolean L-matrix (locator matrix)** that maps local DOFs to global DOFs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Each off-diagonal term $K_{ij}$ physically represents the **stiffness coupling** between nodes $i$ and $j$. A value of $K_{ij} = 0$ means the nodes are kinematically *decoupled* in that particular DOF direction.

### Step D — Application of Boundary Conditions (BCs)
KTU problems feature one of:
- **Homogeneous Essential BC**: $u_k = 0$ (fixed support) → zero out row $k$ and column $k$, place a **1** on the diagonal.
- **Inhomogeneous Essential BC**: $u_k = u_0 \neq 0$ → modify RHS: $F_i^{new} = F_i - K_{ik} u_0$.
- **Natural BC** (prescribed force $F_k$): Retain in RHS, no matrix modification.

### Step E — Solution of the Linear System
Solve the reduced system:

$$[K_{reduced}]\{u_{free}\} = \{F_{known}\}$$

by **Gauss elimination** (manual) or **LU decomposition** (computer-based — strictly not allowed in KTU ESE pen-and-paper unless asked).

### Step F — Post-Processing (Strain & Stress Recovery)
For each element $e$ with nodes $p, q$:

$$\varepsilon^{(e)} = \frac{u_q - u_p}{L^{(e)}}, \quad \sigma^{(e)} = E^{(e)} \varepsilon^{(e)}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | When to Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $[k^{(e)}] = \dfrac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | 2-node bar element stiffness | Always — first step of any problem |
| 2 | $[K] = \sum \mathcal{L}^\top k \mathcal{L}$ | Global assembly | Step before solving |
| 3 | $\{F\} = [K]\{u\}$ | Equilibrium equation | After assembly |
| 4 | $\{u_{free}\} = [K_{red}]^{-1}\{F_{known}\}$ | Solving for unknowns | After BC application |
| 5 | $\sigma = E \dfrac{u_j - u_i}{L}$ | Element axial stress | Final post-processing step |
| 6 | $\delta^{(e)} = \dfrac{F^{(e)} L^{(e)}}{A^{(e)} E^{(e)}}$ | Element elongation check | Verification step |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The bar assembly procedure is the **algorithmic backbone** of every commercial FEM package (ANSYS Mechanical, ABAQUS/Standard, NASTRAN, SAP2000). Whether you are simulating the **load transfer in a stepped shaft** (mechanical engineering), the **axial forces in a transmission tower** (civil), or the **tendon stresses in a prestressed concrete bridge** (structural), the same sequence — element formulation → assembly → BC → solve → post-process — governs the analysis. Mastery of this workflow is therefore **non-negotiable** for any practicing structural analyst.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

## 3.1 Derivation: Bar Element Stiffness Matrix from Variational Principles

We begin from the **Principle of Minimum Potential Energy** ($\Pi_p = U - W$). For a uniform 1D bar under axial load, the strain energy is:

$$\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L} \sigma \, \varepsilon \, A \, dx \\
  &= \frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L} E \left(\frac{du}{dx}\right)^2 A \, dx
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1 — Shape Function Assumption:**
Assume a **linear displacement field** between the two end nodes:

$$u(x) = N_1(x) \, u_1 + N_2(x) \, u_2$$

where the **linear shape functions** (also called **Hermite shape functions** for this simplest case) are:

$$\begin{aligned}
N_1(x) &= 1 - \frac{x}{L}, \quad N_2(x) = \frac{x}{L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Strain-Displacement Matrix [B]:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon(x) = \frac{du}{dx} &= -\frac{1}{L} u_1 + \frac{1}{L} u_2 \\
                                &= \frac{1}{L}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

So the **strain-displacement matrix** is:

$$[B] = \frac{1}{L}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Constitutive Matrix [D]:**
For 1D linear elastic bar: $[D] = E$ (a scalar).

**Step 4 — Element Stiffness Matrix:**

$$\begin{aligned}
[k^{(e)}] &= \int_{0}^{L} [B]^\top [D] [B] \, A \, dx \\
          &= A E \int_{0}^{L} \frac{1}{L^2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} dx \\
          &= \frac{AE}{L^2} \cdot L \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
          &= \frac{AE}{L} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Derivation completed.** Note the elegant result: the stiffness of a bar is **directly proportional** to its **axial rigidity $AE$** and **inversely proportional** to its **length $L$**.

## 3.2 Comprehensive Worked Example — Stepped Bar (Classic KTU Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (Modeled after KTU University Exam Style):**
> A stepped bar fixed at the left end (Node 1) consists of two segments connected in series. **Element 1** has length $L_1 = L$, area $A_1 = A$, modulus $E_1 = E$. **Element 2** has length $L_2 = 2L$, area $A_2 = 2A$, modulus $E_2 = E$. An axial force **P** is applied at the free right end (Node 3). Determine:
> (i) Global stiffness matrix [K]
> (ii) Nodal displacements $\{u\}$
> (iii) Element stresses $\sigma^{(1)}$ and $\sigma^{(2)}$

**Topology Diagram (Conceptual):**
```
    |--L1 (A, E)--|--L2 (2A, E)--|
    O==========O==============O ====> P
   Node 1    Node 2           Node 3
   (fixed)  (free DOF)        (loaded)
```

### Solution — Step 1: Element Stiffness Matrices

**Element 1** ($L_1 = L$, $A_1 = A$, $E_1 = E$):

$$\begin{aligned}
[k^{(1)}] = \frac{(A)(E)}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Element 2** ($L_2 = 2L$, $A_2 = 2A$, $E_2 = E$):

$$\begin{aligned}
[k^{(2)}] = \frac{(2A)(E)}{2L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

### Solution — Step 2: DOF Connectivity & Locator Matrices

$$\begin{aligned}
\{u^{(1)}\} = \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix}, \quad
\mathcal{L}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\{u^{(2)}\} = \begin{Bmatrix} u_2 \\ u_3 \end{Bmatrix}, \quad
\mathcal{L}^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

### Solution — Step 3: Assembly of [K]

For element 1, contribution to global matrix:

$$\begin{aligned}
K^{(1)} &= \mathcal{L}^{(1)\top} [k^{(1)}] \mathcal{L}^{(1)} \\
        &= \frac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

For element 2, contribution to global matrix:

$$\begin{aligned}
K^{(2)} &= \mathcal{L}^{(2)\top} [k^{(2)}] \mathcal{L}^{(2)} \\
        &= \frac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

Adding contributions:

$$\begin{aligned}
[K] &= K^{(1)} + K^{(2)} = \frac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of [K] Properties:** (a) Symmetric ✓, (b) Singular before BC application (determinant = 0) ✓, (c) Banded with bandwidth = 1 ✓.

### Solution — Step 4: Apply Boundary Condition

Given $u_1 = 0$ (fixed support), partition the system:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{Bmatrix} u_2 \\ u_3 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ P \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

(The $F_2 = 0$ because no external force is applied at the interior node 2; the equilibrium of the internal node is automatically satisfied.)

### Solution — Step 5: Solve the Linear System

Multiply out:

$$\begin{aligned}
2u_2 - u_3 &= \frac{PL}{AE} \quad \cdots (i) \\
-u_2 + u_3 &= \frac{PL}{AE} \quad \cdots (ii)
\end{aligned}$$

**Add equations (i) and (ii):**

$$\begin{aligned}
u_2 &= \frac{2PL}{AE}
\end{aligned}$$

**Substitute into (ii):**

$$\begin{aligned}
u_3 &= \frac{PL}{AE} + u_2 = \frac{PL}{AE} + \frac{2PL}{AE} = \frac{3PL}{AE}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Displacement Vector:**

$$\{u\} = \frac{PL}{AE}\begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{Bmatrix}$$

### Solution — Step 6: Post-Processing — Element Strains & Stresses

**Element 1 strain:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon^{(1)} = \frac{u_2 - u_1}{L_1} = \frac{\frac{2PL}{AE} - 0}{L} = \frac{2P}{AE}
\end{aligned}$$

**Element 1 stress:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma^{(1)} = E_1 \cdot \varepsilon^{(1)} = E \cdot \frac{2P}{AE} = \frac{2P}{A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Element 2 strain:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon^{(2)} = \frac{u_3 - u_2}{L_2} = \frac{\frac{3PL}{AE} - \frac{2PL}{AE}}{2L} = \frac{PL/AE}{2L} = \frac{P}{2AE}
\end{aligned}$$

**Element 2 stress:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma^{(2)} = E_2 \cdot \varepsilon^{(2)} = E \cdot \frac{P}{2AE} = \frac{P}{2A}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equilibrium Check (Sanity Test):**
> Internal force in element 1: $F^{(1)} = \sigma^{(1)} \cdot A_1 = \frac{2P}{A} \cdot A = 2P$
> Internal force in element 2: $F^{(2)} = \sigma^{(2)} \cdot A_2 = \frac{P}{2A} \cdot 2A = P$ ✓
> Equilibrium at node 2: $F^{(1)}$ must equal $F^{(2)}$? **Wait — this reveals a non-equilibrium situation!**
>
> This discrepancy is a **classic KTU trap!** The correct interpretation is: the *internal axial force* $N$ is **uniform** through the bar in equilibrium, but the *stress* differs because $A$ differs. For element 1, $N = \sigma^{(1)} A_1 = 2P$ (mismatch indicates our algebra — let us recheck).
>
> **Corrected equilibrium check:**
> Internal axial force $N = \sigma \cdot A$:
> - Element 1: $N^{(1)} = \frac{2P}{A} \cdot A = 2P$
> - Element 2: $N^{(2)} = \frac{P}{2A} \cdot 2A = P$
>
> This indicates that for **equilibrium at node 2**, the force transmitted should be **P** (same as applied at node 3). Element 1 carrying 2P is incorrect — recheck calculation:
>
> $\sigma^{(1)} = \frac{P}{A}$ (correct: $u_2 = \frac{PL}{AE}$ — recompute from symmetric system)
>
> **Recomputation:** From equation (i): $u_2 = u_3/2$. From (ii): $u_3 - u_2 = \frac{PL}{AE}$, so $u_2 = \frac{PL}{AE}$, $u_3 = \frac{2PL}{AE}$.
>
> **Corrected Final:**
> - $\sigma^{(1)} = E \cdot \frac{u_2 - u_1}{L_1} = E \cdot \frac{PL}{AEL} = \frac{P}{A}$
> - $\sigma^{(2)} = E \cdot \frac{u_3 - u_2}{L_2} = E \cdot \frac{PL/AE}{2L} = \frac{P}{2A}$
> - $N^{(1)} = \sigma^{(1)} A_1 = P$ ✓
> - $N^{(2)} = \sigma^{(2)} A_2 = P$ ✓ — **Equilibrium satisfied!**

The corrected, **equilibrium-consistent** results are:

$$\sigma^{(1)} = \frac{P}{A}, \qquad \sigma^{(2)} = \frac{P}{2A}$$

$$u_2 = \frac{PL}{AE}, \qquad u_3 = \frac{2PL}{AE}$$

## 4.3 Schematic: Global Stiffness Matrix Structure

```
                  Global DOF Index →
                    1      2      3
                 ┌──────┬──────┬──────┐
            1    │ k1   │ -k1  │  0   │   ← Element 1 contributes here
                 ├──────┼──────┼──────┤
Node i     2    │ -k1  │ k1+k2 │ -k2  │   ← Node 2 shared: k1+k2
                 ├──────┼──────┼──────┤
            3    │  0   │ -k2  │  k2  │   ← Element 2 contributes here
                 └──────┴──────┴──────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading:** Notice how the diagonal entry $K_{22}$ receives contributions from **both** elements (it is the shared interior node), while $K_{11}$ and $K_{33}$ receive contributions from only **one** element each (boundary nodes). This is the **direct visual fingerprint of the assembly process**.
