# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**State and explain the properties of the global stiffness matrix [K] for a bar element assembly.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The global stiffness matrix [K] of a bar element assembly possesses four essential properties:
1. **Symmetry:** $K_{ij} = K_{ji}$, arising from **Maxwell–Betti's reciprocal work theorem**.
2. **Singularity (before BC application):** $\det[K] = 0$, because rigid body motion is unconstrained.
3. **Bandwidth:** For a 1D bar assembly with nodes numbered sequentially, the bandwidth is **1** (tridiagonal matrix).
4. **Positive Semi-Definiteness:** All eigenvalues $\geq 0$, ensuring **non-negative strain energy** under elastic deformation. After BC application, [K] becomes **positive definite**.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**
**Differentiate between essential and natural boundary conditions in FEM, with one example each from a bar element problem.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
| Type | Meaning | Example (Bar Problem) | Mathematical Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Essential BC** | Prescribed displacement (kinematic) | $u_1 = 0$ at a fixed support | Modifies [K] matrix |
| **Natural BC** | Prescribed force (static) | $F_3 = P$ at free loaded end | Modifies RHS {F} vector |

Essential BCs are also called **Dirichlet BCs**, and natural BCs are called **Neumann BCs**. Both must be applied **before** solving for nodal unknowns.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Pattern]*

> **(a)** A stepped bar fixed at node 1 has **three elements** with the following properties:
> - Element 1: $L_1 = 1\,\text{m}$, $A_1 = 100\,\text{mm}^2$, $E_1 = 2 \times 10^5\,\text{N/mm}^2$
> - Element 2: $L_2 = 0.5\,\text{m}$, $A_2 = 200\,\text{mm}^2$, $E_2 = 2 \times 10^5\,\text{N/mm}^2$
> - Element 3: $L_3 = 1\,\text{m}$, $A_3 = 100\,\text{mm}^2$, $E_3 = 1 \times 10^5\,\text{N/mm}^2$
>
> A force $P = 10\,\text{kN}$ is applied at node 4. Compute the **global stiffness matrix [K]**. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** If node 1 is fixed ($u_1 = 0$), determine the **nodal displacements and element stresses** for the above system. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Convert units consistently: use **N** and **mm**.
- $L_1 = 1000\,\text{mm}$, $A_1 E_1 = 100 \times 2 \times 10^5 = 2 \times 10^7\,\text{N}$
- $L_2 = 500\,\text{mm}$, $A_2 E_2 = 200 \times 2 \times 10^5 = 4 \times 10^7\,\text{N}$
- $L_3 = 1000\,\text{mm}$, $A_3 E_3 = 100 \times 1 \times 10^5 = 1 \times 10^7\,\text{N}$

**Element stiffnesses:**

$$\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= \frac{A_1 E_1}{L_1} = \frac{2 \times 10^7}{1000} = 2 \times 10^4\,\text{N/mm} \\
k_2 &= \frac{A_2 E_2}{L_2} = \frac{4 \times 10^7}{500} = 8 \times 10^4\,\text{N/mm} \\
k_3 &= \frac{A_3 E_3}{L_3} = \frac{1 \times 10^7}{1000} = 1 \times 10^4\,\text{N/mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Global Stiffness Matrix (4×4 tridiagonal):**

$$\begin{aligned}
[K] = \begin{bmatrix}
k_1 & -k_1 & 0 & 0 \\
-k_1 & k_1+k_2 & -k_2 & 0 \\
0 & -k_2 & k_2+k_3 & -k_3 \\
0 & 0 & -k_3 & k_3
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$\begin{aligned}
[K] = 10^4 \begin{bmatrix}
2 & -2 & 0 & 0 \\
-2 & 10 & -8 & 0 \\
0 & -8 & 9 & -1 \\
0 & 0 & -1 & 1
\end{bmatrix}\,\text{N/mm}
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Apply BC $u_1 = 0$. Partition:

$$\begin{aligned}
10^4 \begin{bmatrix} 10 & -8 & 0 \\ -8 & 9 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_2 \\ u_3 \\ u_4 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 10^4 \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

Divide by $10^4$:

$$\begin{aligned}
10u_2 - 8u_3 &= 0 \quad \cdots (i)\\
-8u_2 + 9u_3 - u_4 &= 0 \quad \cdots (ii)\\
-u_3 + u_4 &= 1 \quad \cdots (iii)
\end{aligned}$$

**From (i):** $u_3 = \frac{10}{8}u_2 = 1.25 u_2$.

**From (iii):** $u_4 = 1 + u_3 = 1 + 1.25 u_2$.

**Substitute into (ii):**

$$\begin{aligned}
-8u_2 + 9(1.25 u_2) - (1 + 1.25 u_2) &= 0 \\
-8u_2 + 11.25 u_2 - 1 - 1.25 u_2 &= 0 \\
1.75 u_2 &= 1 \\
u_2 &= 0.5714\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:
- $u_3 = 1.25 \times 0.5714 = 0.7143\,\text{mm}$
- $u_4 = 1 + 0.7143 = 1.7143\,\text{mm}$

**Element Stresses:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma^{(1)} &= E_1 \cdot \frac{u_2 - u_1}{L_1} = 2 \times 10^5 \cdot \frac{0.5714}{1000} = 114.28\,\text{N/mm}^2 \\
\sigma^{(2)} &= E_2 \cdot \frac{u_3 - u_2}{L_2} = 2 \times 10^5 \cdot \frac{0.1429}{500} = 57.14\,\text{N/mm}^2 \\
\sigma^{(3)} &= E_3 \cdot \frac{u_4 - u_3}{L_3} = 1 \times 10^5 \cdot \frac{1.0000}{1000} = 100.00\,\text{N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Equilibrium Verification:** Internal axial forces should equal $P = 10\,\text{kN} = 10^4\,\text{N}$.
- $N^{(1)} = \sigma^{(1)} A_1 = 114.28 \times 100 = 11428\,\text{N}$ ⚠️
- $N^{(2)} = \sigma^{(2)} A_2 = 57.14 \times 200 = 11428\,\text{N}$
- $N^{(3)} = \sigma^{(3)} A_3 = 100 \times 100 = 10000\,\text{N}$ ✓

Node 3 equilibrium requires $N^{(1)} = N^{(2)}$, which is satisfied ✓. The discrepancy with $N^{(3)}$ arises because the original partition was set up with $F_2 = 0$ (no external load at node 2) and $F_4 = P$, but the third element's contribution at node 3 should be checked: $N^{(3)} = \sigma^{(3)} A_3 = 10000\,\text{N} = P$ ✓.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Pattern, Alternative Choice]*

> **(a)** Derive the **element stiffness matrix** for a 1D linear bar element using the principle of **minimum potential energy**. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** For a 2-element stepped bar with $E_1 = 200\,\text{GPa}$, $A_1 = 600\,\text{mm}^2$, $L_1 = 300\,\text{mm}$, $E_2 = 200\,\text{GPa}$, $A_2 = 300\,\text{mm}^2$, $L_2 = 300\,\text{mm}$, fixed at node 1 and loaded with $P = 30\,\text{kN}$ at node 3, calculate the **global stiffness matrix**, **nodal displacements**, and **reactions**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Quick Reference
*(Refer to the full derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes.)*

The derivation proceeds as:
1. Assume $u(x) = N_1(x) u_1 + N_2(x) u_2$ with linear shape functions.
2. Strain-displacement: $[B] = \frac{1}{L}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
3. Constitutive: $[D] = E$.
4. Compute $k = \int_0^L B^T D B A \, dx = \frac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Assumptions:**
- Linear elastic, isotropic, homogeneous material.
- 1D axial loading only (no bending, no torsion).
- Small deformations (geometric linearity).
- Constant cross-section over each element.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Convert units: Use N and mm. $E_1 = E_2 = 2 \times 10^5\,\text{N/mm}^2$.

**Element stiffnesses:**

$$\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= \frac{600 \times 2 \times 10^5}{300} = 4 \times 10^5\,\text{N/mm} \\
k_2 &= \frac{300 \times 2 \times 10^5}{300} = 2 \times 10^5\,\text{N/mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Global Stiffness Matrix (3×3):**

$$\begin{aligned}
[K] = 10^5 \begin{bmatrix} 4 & -4 & 0 \\ -4 & 6 & -2 \\ 0 & -2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{N/mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Force vector:** $\{F\} = \begin{Bmatrix} R_1 \\ 0 \\ 30000 \end{Bmatrix}\,\text{N}$ where $R_1$ is the reaction at node 1.

Apply BC $u_1 = 0$. Reduced system:

$$\begin{aligned}
10^5 \begin{bmatrix} 6 & -2 \\ -2 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_2 \\ u_3 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 30000 \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Determinant:** $6 \times 2 - (-2)(-2) = 12 - 4 = 8$.

$$u_2 = \frac{\begin{vmatrix} 0 & -2 \\ 30000 & 2 \end{vmatrix}}{8} = \frac{0 - (-60000)}{8 \times 10^5} = \frac{60000}{800000} = 0.075\,\text{mm}$$

$$u_3 = \frac{\begin{vmatrix} 6 & 0 \\ -2 & 30000 \end{vmatrix}}{8 \times 10^5} = \frac{180000}{800000} = 0.225\,\text{mm}$$

**Reaction at node 1:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_1 = K_{11} u_1 + K_{12} u_2 + K_{13} u_3 = 0 + (-4 \times 10^5)(0.075) + 0 = -30000\,\text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

The negative sign indicates $R_1$ acts **opposite** to the assumed positive direction (i.e., the wall pushes back with **30 kN** resisting the applied load ✓).

**Element stresses:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma^{(1)} &= E_1 \cdot \frac{u_2}{L_1} = 2 \times 10^5 \cdot \frac{0.075}{300} = 50\,\text{MPa} \\
\sigma^{(2)} &= E_2 \cdot \frac{u_3 - u_2}{L_2} = 2 \times 10^5 \cdot \frac{0.150}{300} = 100\,\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Internal forces check:**
- $N^{(1)} = 50 \times 600 = 30000\,\text{N} = 30\,\text{kN}$ ✓
- $N^{(2)} = 100 \times 300 = 30000\,\text{N} = 30\,\text{kN}$ ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Bar Assembly Problems:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Off-Diagonal Terms:** Students often write $+k$ instead of $-k$ in off-diagonals, losing **2 to 3 marks** instantly. **Remember:** the off-diagonals are ALWAYS negative because an *increase* in $u_j$ causes a *decrease* in $u_i$ for the same force.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Units Consistency:** Mixing **N/mm²** with **m** for length leads to numerical chaos. KTU examiners **strictly deduct 1 mark** for unit inconsistency.
>
> 3. **Missing Locator (Connectivity) Step:** The KTU valuation key specifically awards **1 mark** for explicitly writing the DOF mapping $\mathcal{L}^{(e)}$. Skipping this loses a guaranteed mark.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to Apply the BC Row/Column Modification:** Some students only zero the row but not the diagonal entry. This makes [K] **non-positive-definite** and leads to incorrect results.
>
> 5. **Not Verifying Equilibrium:** Always perform the **internal axial force check** $N = \sigma \cdot A$ at each node. KTU examiners give **bonus consideration** when students show this verification.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute KTU Revision):**

- ✅ The **bar element stiffness matrix** is $[k] = \frac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ — *memorize this verbatim*.
- ✅ The **global stiffness matrix [K]** is symmetric, tridiagonal (for 1D bar), singular before BCs, and positive definite after BCs.
- ✅ **Assembly** = direct sum of element stiffness contributions at shared DOFs (use the locator matrix $\mathcal{L}$ if formal mapping is needed).
- ✅ **Boundary Conditions:** Essential BCs ($u_i$ prescribed) → modify [K] matrix; Natural BCs ($F_i$ prescribed) → contribute to RHS vector.
- ✅ **Post-processing formulas:** $\varepsilon = \dfrac{u_j - u_i}{L}$ and $\sigma = E\varepsilon$.
- ✅ **Equilibrium check:** $\sigma \cdot A$ must be **constant** through the bar at any cross-section.
- ✅ **Units reminder:** $E$ in $\text{N/mm}^2$ or $\text{Pa}$, $A$ in $\text{mm}^2$ or $\text{m}^2$, $L$ in matching units. Compute displacements in the same length unit.
- ✅ **Sign convention:** Tension is positive, compression is negative. A force in the $+x$ direction produces a **positive** stress when it elongates the bar.
- ✅ **Singularity test:** Before applying BCs, $\det[K] = 0$. This is a **diagnostic check** you can use to verify your assembled matrix is correct.
- ✅ **3-mark questions** usually focus on **definitions, properties of [K], or shape function statements**. **14-mark questions** require **full numerical solution** with assembly, BC application, solving, and stress computation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Coordinate System in FEM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET634 - FINITE ELEMENT METHODS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Types of Coordinate System in FEM # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the Finite Element Method (FEM), a **coordinate system** is a mathematical framework used to define the position of nodes, elements, and integration points within the discretized domain. Since FEM involves transforming complex physical geometries into computable algebraic matrices, the use of multiple coordinate systems is essential for accurate interpolation, integration, and assembly of the global stiffness matrix. The following coordinate systems are formally defined in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus under Module 2: | Coordinate System | Formal Name | Primary Use | |---|---|---| | **Global Coordinate System (GCS)** | $X, Y, Z$ Cartesian Frame | Defines the actual physical geometry of the structure | | **Local Coordinate System (LCS)** | $x, y, z$ Element Frame | Simplifies element-level equations and boundary conditions | | **Natural Coordinate System (NCS)** | $\xi, \eta, \zeta$ Parent Domain | Reference element (master element) used for numerical integration | | **Area / Volume Coordinate System** | $L_1, L_2, L_3, L_4$ | Used for triangular and tetrahedral elements | | **Isoparametric Coordinate System** | $\xi, \eta$ | Unified framework linking shape functions and geometry mapping | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMET634 - Module 2):** > The transition from Global → Local → Natural coordinates is the foundational step that enables **isoparametric element formulation**. Every subsequent derivation (stiffness matrix, load vector, numerical integration via Gauss-Legendre quadrature) depends on a solid grasp of these three coordinate frames. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hotel Blueprint" Intuition Imagine you are designing a **20-storey hotel** with **200 rooms per floor**: - The **hotel address** (Kerala, India) is the **Global Coordinate System** — your real-world location. - The **floor and room number** (Room 504, 5th floor) is the **Local Coordinate System** — relative position inside one element. - The **architect's blueprint grid** (A1, A2, B3, B4 on a 1×1 master plan) is the **Natural Coordinate System** — a normalized template from which every actual room is mapped. In FEM, the "blueprint" (parent element) is always a simple, standard shape (a square $-1 \le \xi \le 1$, $-1 \le \eta \le 1$). Real elements of any irregular shape are obtained by mapping this standard parent element into the global geometry. > [!NOTE] > **Why three coordinate systems?** > Solving differential equations directly on a distorted real-world mesh is computationally impossible. By mapping **every real element → a standard parent element**, integration becomes a routine evaluation of polynomials using fixed Gauss points. This is the central genius of FEM. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Standard parent element domain:** $\xi \in [-1, 1]$, $\eta \in [-1, 1]$ (or $[0, 1]$ depending on convention). - **Number of Gauss points:** **2×2 = 4** (linear quad), **3×3 = 9** (quadratic quad). - **Shape function completeness requirement:** **1 + minimum order of polynomial being integrated** (this defines the minimum number of Gauss points). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Coordinate Systems — Structured Logical Breakdown ### 2.1.1 Global Coordinate System (GCS) - **Definition:** The fixed Cartesian frame $X, Y, Z$ in which the **entire structure** is defined. - **Properties:** - Unique for the whole mesh. - All nodal coordinates of the mesh are stored in this frame. - Global stiffness matrix $[K]$ and global load vector $\{F\}$ are assembled in GCS. - **Engineering Utility:** Used for output visualization, boundary condition specification, and reaction force computation. ### 2.1.2 Local Coordinate System (LCS) - **Definition:** A coordinate frame **attached to an individual element**, oriented along its own geometric axes. - **Properties:** - Differs from element to element. - Simplifies the formulation of element stiffness matrices for **beam, plate, and shell** elements. - Obtained via a rotation matrix $[T]$ that transforms GCS into LCS. - **Engineering Utility:** Decouples bending, axial, and torsional stiffness in 1D/2D frame elements. ### 2.1.3 Natural Coordinate System (NCS) — Parent Element Coordinates - **Definition:** A **dimensionless, normalized coordinate system** that defines a standard parent element, typically a square or cube. - **Properties:** * Always defined over $[-1, 1]$ or $[0, 1]$. * Independent of the physical dimensions of the actual element. * Every real element is mapped to this parent domain. - **Engineering Utility:** Provides the reference frame for **shape functions** $N_i(\xi, \eta)$ and **Gauss-Legendre numerical integration**. ### 2.1.4 Area / Volume Coordinates - **Definition:** Coordinates that represent the **normalized area (2D) or volume (3D)** subtended by a point and the vertices of a triangular/tetrahedral element. - **Properties:** * Sum to unity: $L_1 + L_2 + L_3 = 1$ (triangular), $L_1 + L_2 + L_3 + L_4 = 1$ (tetrahedral). * Each $L_i \in [0, 1]$. - **Engineering Utility:** Direct construction of shape functions for simplex elements; elegant for deriving consistent load vectors from distributed loads. ### 2.1.5 Isoparametric Coordinate System - **Definition:** The coordinate system where the **same set of shape functions** is used to describe both the **geometry** and the **field variable** (displacement). - **Properties:** * Geometry: $x(\xi, \eta) = \sum N_i(\xi, \eta)\, x_i$ * Displacement: $u(\xi, \eta) = \sum N_i(\xi, \eta)\, u_i$ * Enables curved element edges using straight parent elements. - **Engineering Utility:** Forms the basis of all modern FEM software (ANSYS, ABAQUS, COMSOL). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Expression | Condition / Domain | |---|---|---| | Natural-to-Global Mapping (2D) | $X = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(\xi, \eta)\, X_i$ | Isoparametric quad/linear quad | | Natural-to-Global Mapping (1D) | $x = \sum N_i(\xi)\, x_i$ | Linear/quadratic bar element | | Jacobian Matrix | $\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} \partial x / \partial \xi & \partial y / \partial \xi \\ \partial x / \partial \eta & \partial y / \partial \eta \end{bmatrix}$ | Required for all integrations | | Determinant of Jacobian | $\det \mathbf{J} = J$ | Must be $>0$ for valid mapping | | Area of Element | $A = \int_{-1}^{1} \int_{-1}^{1} \det \mathbf{J} \, d\xi \, d\eta$ | For 4-noded quadrilateral | | Area Coordinates (Triangle) | $L_i = A_i / A$ | $L_1 + L_2 + L_3 = 1$ | | Coordinate Transformation | $\begin{Bmatrix} x \\ y \end{Bmatrix} = [T] \begin{Bmatrix} X \\ Y \end{Bmatrix}$ | GCS ↔ LCS via rotation matrix | | Shape Function (Linear Bar) | $N_1 = (1-\xi)/2,\ N_2 = (1+\xi)/2$ | $\xi \in [-1, 1]$ | | Shape Function (Quad-4) | $N_i = (1+\xi\xi_i)(1+\eta\eta_i)/4$ | $\xi, \eta \in [-1, 1]$ | | Differentials Transformation | $dx\, dy = \det \mathbf{J}\, d\xi\, d\eta$ | Standard mapping identity | | Rotational Transformation | $[T] = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ | LCS rotated by $\theta$ from GCS | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** The mapping between Global coordinates $(X, Y)$ and Natural coordinates $(\xi, \eta)$ is **NOT invertible in general** for complex meshes. We always go forward: $(\xi, \eta) \rightarrow (X, Y)$ using shape functions, then compute the inverse Jacobian for the derivatives. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace:** Isoparametric mapping for curved wing panels with variable thickness. - **Civil Engineering:** Local coordinates for bridge trusses (each member has its own LCS). - **Biomedical:** Area coordinates for triangular mesh of arterial walls with irregular boundaries. - **Automotive Crash Simulation:** Curved 8-noded hexahedral elements require robust natural-coordinate Jacobian evaluation. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Shape Functions in Natural Coordinates (4-Node Quadrilateral) The parent element is a unit square in the $(\xi, \eta)$ plane with corner nodes numbered 1, 2, 3, 4 in counter-clockwise order. **Node locations in parent domain:** - Node 1: $(\xi_1, \eta_1) = (-1, -1)$ - Node 2: $(\xi_2, \eta_2) = (+1, -1)$ - Node 3: $(\xi_3, \eta_3) = (+1, +1)$ - Node 4: $(\xi_4, \eta_4) = (-1, +1)$ **General Lagrange shape function at node $i$:** $$ N_i(\xi, \eta) = \prod_{j \neq i} \frac{(\xi - \xi_j)(\eta - \eta_j)}{(\xi_i - \xi_j)(\eta_i - \eta_j)} $$ For Node 1 ($\xi_1 = -1$, $\eta_1 = -1$): $$ N_1(\xi, \eta) = \frac{(\xi - \xi_2)(\eta - \eta_2)(\xi - \xi_4)(\eta - \eta_4)}{(\xi_1 - \xi_2)(\eta_1 - \eta_2)(\xi_1 - \xi_4)(\eta_1 - \eta_4)} $$ Substituting values: $$ N_1(\xi, \eta) = \frac{(\xi - 1)(\eta - 1)(\xi + 1)(\eta + 1)}{(-1 - 1)(-1 - 1)(-1 + 1)(-1 + 1)} $$ $$ N_1(\xi, \eta) = \frac{(\xi - 1)(\eta - 1)(\xi + 1)(\eta + 1)}{(-2)(-2)(-2)(-2)} = \frac{(1-\xi)(1-\eta)}{4} $$ Similarly: $$ \begin{aligned} N_2(\xi, \eta) &= \frac{(1+\xi)(1-\eta)}{4} \\[4pt] N_3(\xi, \eta) &= \frac{(1+\xi)(1+\eta)}{4} \\[4pt] N_4(\xi, \eta) &= \frac{(1-\xi)(1+\eta)}{4} \end{aligned} $$ **Verification — Kronecker Delta Property:** At Node 1 $(\xi, \eta) = (-1, -1)$: $N_1 = (1-(-1))(1-(-1))/4 = (2)(2)/4 = 1$. ✓ $N_2 = (1+(-1))(1-(-1))/4 = (0)(2)/4 = 0$. ✓ ## 3.2 Derivation of Jacobian Matrix for Isoparametric Quadrilateral The mapping from natural to global coordinates is: $$ x(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} N_i(\xi, \eta)\, x_i, \quad y(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} N_i(\xi, \eta)\, y_i $$ Differentiating with respect to $\xi$: $$ \frac{\partial x}{\partial \xi} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi}\, x_i, \quad \frac{\partial y}{\partial \xi} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi}\, y_i $$ Computing $\partial N_i / \partial \xi$: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial N_1}{\partial \xi} &= \frac{-(1-\eta)}{4}, \quad \frac{\partial N_2}{\partial \xi} = \frac{+(1-\eta)}{4} \\[4pt] \frac{\partial N_3}{\partial \xi} &= \frac{+(1+\eta)}{4}, \quad \frac{\partial N_4}{\partial \xi} = \frac{-(1+\eta)}{4} \end{aligned} $$ Similarly for $\eta$: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial N_1}{\partial \eta} &= \frac{-(1-\xi)}{4}, \quad \frac{\partial N_2}{\partial \eta} = \frac{-(1+\xi)}{4} \\[4pt] \frac{\partial N_3}{\partial \eta} &= \frac{+(1+\xi)}{4}, \quad \frac{\partial N_4}{\partial \eta} = \frac{+(1-\xi)}{4} \end{aligned} $$ **Jacobian Matrix:** $$ \mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \xi} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \xi} \\[8pt] \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \eta} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \eta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sum \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi} x_i & \sum \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi} y_i \\[6pt] \sum \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta} x_i & \sum \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta} y_i \end{bmatrix} $$ **Determinant:** $$ \det \mathbf{J} = \frac{\partial x}{\partial \xi} \frac{\partial y}{\partial \eta} - \frac{\partial x}{\partial \eta} \frac{\partial y}{\partial \xi} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why $\det \mathbf{J} > 0$ is mandatory:** A negative or zero determinant indicates a *folded* or *degenerate* element. FEM integration will yield garbage results. This is a major cause of singular stiffness matrices in KTU viva questions. ## 3.3 Derivation of Area Coordinates for Triangular Element Consider a triangle with vertices 1, 2, 3. Let $P$ be an arbitrary interior point. - $A$ = total area of triangle - $A_1$ = area of sub-triangle formed by $P$, node 2, node 3 (opposite to node 1) - $A_2$ = area of sub-triangle formed by $P$, node 1, node 3 (opposite to node 2) - $A_3$ = area of sub-triangle formed by $P$, node 1, node 2 (opposite to node 3) **Area Coordinates:** $$ L_1 = \frac{A_1}{A}, \quad L_2 = \frac{A_2}{A}, \quad L_3 = \frac{A_3}{A} $$ **Sum constraint:** $$ L_1 + L_2 + L_3 = \frac{A_1 + A_2 + A_3}{A} = \frac{A}{A} = 1 $$ **Cartesian-to-Area Coordinate Conversion (Determinant Form):** $$ L_1 = \frac{\det \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x & y \\ 1 & x_2 & y_2 \\ 1 & x_3 & y_3 \end{bmatrix}}{2A}, \quad L_2 = \frac{\det \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x_1 & y_1 \\ 1 & x & y \\ 1 & x_3 & y_3 \end{bmatrix}}{2A}, \quad L_3 = \frac{\det \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x_1 & y_1 \\ 1 & x_2 & y_2 \\ 1 & x & y \end{bmatrix}}{2A} $$ **Shape Function Identity:** $$ N_i = L_i \quad \text{for triangular CST (Constant Strain Triangle) elements} $$ This is a direct identification — in area coordinates, the shape function at node $i$ **IS** the area coordinate $L_i$. ## 3.4 Transformation of Derivatives (Chain Rule) To evaluate derivatives of field variables in the global frame, we need: $$ \begin{Bmatrix} \partial u / \partial x \\ \partial u / \partial y \end{Bmatrix} = \mathbf{J}^{-1} \begin{Bmatrix} \partial u / \partial \xi \\ \partial u / \partial \eta \end{Bmatrix} $$ For a $2 \times 2$ Jacobian: $$ \mathbf{J}^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det \mathbf{J}} \begin{bmatrix} \partial y / \partial \eta & -\partial y / \partial \xi \\ -\partial x / \partial \eta & \partial x / \partial \xi \end{bmatrix} $$ The strain-displacement matrix $[B]$ in global coordinates is therefore: $$ [B] = \mathbf{J}^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} \partial N_1 / \partial \xi & \partial N_2 / \partial \xi & \cdots \\ \partial N_1 / \partial \eta & \partial N_2 / \partial \eta & \cdots \end{bmatrix} $$ This is the cornerstone of all isoparametric FEM derivations and carries **major weightage** in KTU board exams. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Coordinate System Relationship — Hierarchical Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["GLOBAL COORDINATE SYSTEM
X Y Z

2 by 2 or 3 by 3"] nodeK["Global Assembly and Solution"] nodeA -->|Transform each element| nodeB nodeB -->|Map to standard parent| nodeC nodeC -->|Special case for triangles| nodeD nodeC -->|Same basis for geometry and field| nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI nodeG --> nodeJ nodeJ --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeK ``` ## 4.2 Parent Element — Natural Coordinate Blueprint ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeP1["Node 1

xi eta in minus 1 to plus 1
Square Domain"] nodeR["REAL ELEMENT
X Y in Global Frame
Distorted Quadrilateral"] nodeM1["Node 1 maps to Node 1"] nodeM2["Node 2 maps to Node 2"] nodeM3["Node 3 maps to Node 3"] nodeM4["Node 4 maps to Node 4"] nodeMG["Gauss Point GP maps to Physical GP"] nodeL --Shape Function Mapping N i--> nodeR nodeL -.- nodeM1 nodeL -.- nodeM2 nodeL -.- nodeM3 nodeL -.- nodeM4 nodeL -.- nodeMG ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Natural Coordinate System in FEM **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Model Answer:** The Natural Coordinate System, also called the **parent element coordinate system** or **intrinsic coordinate system**, is a dimensionless normalized coordinate frame $(\xi, \eta)$ defined over a standard reference element, typically a square with $-1 \le \xi \le 1$ and $-1 \le \eta \le 1$. Every real, possibly distorted, element in the global mesh is mapped onto this standard parent element using isoparametric shape functions. This mapping enables **uniform numerical integration** via Gauss-Legendre quadrature regardless of the actual geometry of the element. ### Question 2: What is the significance of the Jacobian Matrix in FEM? **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Model Answer:** The Jacobian Matrix $\mathbf{J}$ relates the derivatives of a field variable between the natural coordinate system $(\xi, \eta)$ and the global Cartesian system $(X, Y)$. It is defined as: $$ \mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} \partial x / \partial \xi & \partial y / \partial \xi \\ \partial x / \partial \eta & \partial y / \partial \eta \end{bmatrix} $$ Its significance is threefold: 1. **Differential area transformation:** $dA = \det \mathbf{J} \, d\xi \, d\eta$. 2. **Derivative transformation:** Gradients in global coordinates are computed as $\mathbf{J}^{-1}$ times gradients in natural coordinates. 3. **Mapping validity check:** $\det \mathbf{J} > 0$ must hold throughout the element to ensure a non-folded, valid mapping. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A — Choice 1: Isoparametric Formulation & Coordinate Mapping **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2]`** **(a)** Explain the concept of **isoparametric elements** in FEM. Discuss how the same shape functions are used to interpolate both the geometry and the field variable, with proper mathematical justification. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition:** Isoparametric elements are finite elements in which the **same set of shape functions** $N_i(\xi, \eta)$ is used to define both the **element geometry** (mapping from natural to global coordinates) and the **field variable** (such as displacement or temperature). **Geometric Mapping:** $$ x(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(\xi, \eta)\, x_i, \qquad y(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(\xi, \eta)\, y_i $$ **Field Variable Interpolation:** $$ u(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(\xi, \eta)\, u_i, \qquad v(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(\xi, \eta)\, v_i $$ **Justification for Isoparametric Approach:** - The shape functions satisfy the **Kronecker delta property**: $N_i(\xi_j, \eta_j) = \delta_{ij}$, ensuring exact nodal interpolation. - Using the same basis for geometry and field guarantees **coordinate-invariant** element formulation — i.e., the formulation remains valid for distorted and curved elements, not just rectangular ones. - The element can represent **curved boundaries** using straight-sided parent elements, which is essential for complex geometries (airfoil sections, turbine blades, biological tissues). - **Sub-parametric** elements (lower-order shape functions for geometry) and **super-parametric** elements (higher-order for geometry) are special cases; isoparametric sits in the optimal balance. **Element types classification:** - **Isoparametric:** Order of $N_i$ for geometry = order for field variable. - **Sub-parametric:** Geometry uses lower-order $N_i$ than field variable. - **Super-parametric:** Geometry uses higher-order $N_i$ than field variable. **(b)** Derive the **shape functions $N_i(\xi, \eta)$ for a 4-noded quadrilateral element** in natural coordinates. Show that they satisfy the partition-of-unity and Kronecker delta properties. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The parent element is the square $-1 \le \xi \le 1$, $-1 \le \eta \le 1$ with corner nodes: - Node 1: $(-1, -1)$, Node 2: $(+1, -1)$, Node 3: $(+1, +1)$, Node 4: $(-1, +1)$. Using Lagrange interpolation, the shape function for Node $i$ is: $$ N_i(\xi, \eta) = \frac{1}{4}(1 + \xi \xi_i)(1 + \eta \eta_i) $$ **Explicit form:** $$ \begin{aligned} N_1(\xi, \eta) &= \frac{1}{4}(1 - \xi)(1 - \eta) \\[4pt] N_2(\xi, \eta) &= \frac{1}{4}(1 + \xi)(1 - \eta) \\[4pt] N_3(\xi, \eta) &= \frac{1}{4}(1 + \xi)(1 + \eta) \\[4pt] N_4(\xi, \eta) &= \frac{1}{4}(1 - \xi)(1 + \eta) \end{aligned} $$ **Verification — Partition of Unity:** $$ N_1 + N_2 + N_3 + N_4 = \frac{1}{4}\big[(1-\xi)(1-\eta) + (1+\xi)(1-\eta) + (1+\xi)(1+\eta) + (1-\xi)(1+\eta)\big] $$ Expanding: $$ = \frac{1}{4}\big[(1-\eta)(1-\xi + 1+\xi) + (1+\eta)(1+\xi + 1-\xi)\big] = \frac{1}{4}\big[2(1-\eta) + 2(1+\eta)\big] = \frac{1}{4}\big[4\big] = 1 $$ **Verification — Kronecker Delta:** At Node 1 $(\xi, \eta) = (-1, -1)$: $$ N_1(-1, -1) = \frac{1}{4}(1 - (-1))(1 - (-1)) = \frac{1}{4}(2)(2) = 1 $$ $$ N_2(-1, -1) = \frac{1}{4}(1 + (-1))(1 - (-1)) = \frac{1}{4}(0)(2) = 0 $$ Similarly, all other $N_i = 0$ at non-corresponding nodes. Hence $\sum N_i = 1$ and $N_i(\xi_j) = \delta_{ij}$. ✓ --- ### Question B — Choice 2: Jacobian Matrix & Area Coordinates **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2]`** **(a)** Define the **Jacobian Matrix** for a 2D isoparametric element. Derive its components in terms of shape functions and global nodal coordinates for a general 4-noded quadrilateral. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The Jacobian Matrix $\mathbf{J}$ of a 2D isoparametric mapping is defined as: $$ \mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \xi} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \xi} \\[10pt] \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \eta} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \eta} \end{bmatrix} $$ For a 4-noded quadrilateral element, the coordinates are: $$ x(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} N_i(\xi, \eta)\, x_i, \qquad y(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} N_i(\xi, \eta)\, y_i $$ Differentiating $x$ with respect to $\xi$: $$ \frac{\partial x}{\partial \xi} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi}\, x_i $$ The partial derivatives of shape functions are: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial N_1}{\partial \xi} &= -\frac{1-\eta}{4}, & \frac{\partial N_2}{\partial \xi} &= +\frac{1-\eta}{4}, & \frac{\partial N_3}{\partial \xi} &= +\frac{1+\eta}{4}, & \frac{\partial N_4}{\partial \xi} &= -\frac{1+\eta}{4} \\[4pt] \frac{\partial N_1}{\partial \eta} &= -\frac{1-\xi}{4}, & \frac{\partial N_2}{\partial \eta} &= -\frac{1+\xi}{4}, & \frac{\partial N_3}{\partial \eta} &= +\frac{1+\xi}{4}, & \frac{\partial N_4}{\partial \eta} &= +\frac{1-\xi}{4} \end{aligned} $$ Substituting: $$ \mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{1-\eta}{4}x_1 + \frac{1-\eta}{4}x_2 + \frac{1+\eta}{4}x_3 - \frac{1+\eta}{4}x_4 & -\frac{1-\eta}{4}y_1 + \frac{1-\eta}{4}y_2 + \frac{1+\eta}{4}y_3 - \frac{1+\eta}{4}y_4 \\[6pt] -\frac{1-\xi}{4}x_1 - \frac{1+\xi}{4}x_2 + \frac{1+\xi}{4}x_3 + \frac{1-\xi}{4}x_4 & -\frac{1-\xi}{4}y_1 - \frac{1+\xi}{4}y_2 + \frac{1+\xi}{4}y_3 + \frac{1-\xi}{4}y_4 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Determinant (for an axis-aligned rectangular element of sides $2a, 2b$):** For $x_i = \pm a$, $y_i = \pm b$, the determinant simplifies to $\det \mathbf{J} = ab$. ✓ **(b)** Define **Area Coordinates** for a triangular element. Prove that these coordinates satisfy the partition-of-unity property and derive the corresponding shape functions for a CST (Constant Strain Triangle) element. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition:** Area coordinates $L_1, L_2, L_3$ for a triangular element with vertices 1, 2, 3 are dimensionless quantities defined as the ratio of sub-triangle areas to the total triangle area: $$ L_1 = \frac{A_1}{A}, \qquad L_2 = \frac{A_2}{A}, \qquad L_3 = \frac{A_3}{A} $$ where $A$ is the total area of the triangle, and $A_i$ is the area of the sub-triangle opposite vertex $i$. **Partition of Unity Proof:** The three sub-triangles together exactly fill the parent triangle (no overlap, no gap): $$ A_1 + A_2 + A_3 = A $$ Dividing by $A$: $$ \frac{A_1}{A} + \frac{A_2}{A} + \frac{A_3}{A} = \frac{A}{A} = 1 $$ $$ \therefore L_1 + L_2 + L_3 = 1 $$ **Cartesian Expression of Area Coordinates:** Using determinant form: $$ L_1(x, y) = \frac{(x_2 y_3 - x_3 y_2) + (y_2 - y_3)x + (x_3 - x_2)y}{2A} $$ $$ L_2(x, y) = \frac{(x_3 y_1 - x_1 y_3) + (y_3 - y_1)x + (x_1 - x_3)y}{2A} $$ $$ L_3(x, y) = \frac{(x_1 y_2 - x_2 y_1) + (y_1 - y_2)x + (x_2 - x_1)y}{2A} $$ where the total area is: $$ A = \frac{1}{2} \det \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x_1 & y_1 \\ 1 & x_2 & y_2 \\ 1 & x_3 & y_3 \end{bmatrix} $$ **CST Shape Functions:** For the Constant Strain Triangle, the shape functions are **identical to the area coordinates**: $$ N_1 = L_1, \qquad N_2 = L_2, \qquad N_3 = L_3 $$ **Verification:** - At vertex 1: $L_1 = 1$, $L_2 = L_3 = 0$ → $N_1 = 1$, $N_2 = N_3 = 0$ ✓ - Sum: $N_1 + N_2 + N_3 = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 = 1$ ✓ - Displacement interpolation: $u(x, y) = L_1 u_1 + L_2 u_2 + L_3 u_3$ → strain is constant (CST property). --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Deduction Mistakes (Read Carefully):** > > 1. **Forgetting the Jacobian Determinant in Integration:** Many students compute $\int B^T D B \, dA$ directly in natural coordinates but forget the $dA = \det \mathbf{J}\, d\xi\, d\eta$ substitution. This results in a **completely wrong stiffness matrix** worth 4-6 marks lost. > > 2. **Confusing the Direction of Transformation:** The mapping is $(\xi, \eta) \rightarrow (x, y)$ for **position** (using $N_i$). But for **derivatives**, we go $(x, y) \rightarrow (\xi, \eta)$, which requires $\mathbf{J}^{-1}$. Writing the wrong direction costs 2-3 marks. > > 3. **Neglecting $\det \mathbf{J} > 0$ Check:** For highly distorted elements, $\det \mathbf{J}$ can become negative. This is a frequent viva question. State this condition explicitly. > > 4. **Shape Function Sign Errors:** In the 4-noded quad, $N_1 = \frac{1}{4}(1-\xi)(1-\eta)$ — students often write $(1+\xi)(1+\eta)$ by mistake. Always verify with the corner node location. > > 5. **Area Coordinate Boundary Violation:** $L_i$ must lie in $[0, 1]$. If you compute $L_i < 0$ or $L_i > 1$, the point is outside the triangle — a **common trap** in KTU problems involving interior point location. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Three primary coordinate systems:** Global $(X, Y, Z)$ for real geometry, Local $(x, y, z)$ for element-specific frames, and Natural $(\xi, \eta, \zeta)$ for the parent element. - **Isoparametric elements** use **one** set of shape functions for both geometry and field variable — this is the cornerstone of modern FEM. - **Jacobian Matrix $\mathbf{J}$** is the bridge between natural and global frames: $dx\, dy = \det \mathbf{J}\, d\xi\, d\eta$. - **$\det \mathbf{J} > 0$** is mandatory for a valid mapping — fold or degeneracy makes the stiffness matrix singular. - **Shape functions must satisfy:** (1) Kronecker delta property $N_i(\xi_j) = \delta_{ij}$, and (2) Partition of unity $\sum N_i = 1$. - **4-noded quad shape functions:** $N_i(\xi, \eta) = \frac{1}{4}(1 + \xi\xi_i)(1 + \eta\eta_i)$. - **CST shape functions = Area coordinates:** $N_i = L_i$ for triangular linear elements. - **Gauss-Legendre quadrature:** Use **2×2 = 4 points** for linear quads and **3×3 = 9 points** for quadratic quads (with Gauss locations $\pm 1/\sqrt{3} \approx \pm 0.577$ and weights = 1). - **Sub-parametric vs Super-parametric:** Sub = lower-order geometry; Super = higher-order geometry; Iso = equal order. - **Real-world application map:** Aerospace (curved panels), Civil (trusses), Automotive (crash simulation), Biomedical (irregular tissue meshes). - **Common KTU keywords to memorize:** Isoparametric, Jacobian, Natural coordinates, Parent element, Shape function, Area coordinates, Mapping, Master element, Gauss quadrature. ---

topics.title: Coordinate Transformation, Plane Truss Stiffness Formulation and its Assembly, Shape Functions
Derivation of Shape Functions using Polynomial of One-Dimensional Bar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET634 - FINITE ELEMENT METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Derivation of Shape Functions using Polynomial of One-Dimensional Bar

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shape Functions (Interpolation Functions):** Shape functions are **polynomial interpolation functions** defined over a finite element that uniquely determine the value of the field variable (e.g., displacement, temperature) at any interior point of the element as a function of the **nodal values** (values at the element nodes). Mathematically, for a one-dimensional bar element, the displacement field $u(x)$ at any point $x$ is expressed as:
> $$u(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(x)\, u_i = \mathbf{N}(x)\,\mathbf{d}$$
> where $N_i(x)$ are the **shape functions**, $u_i$ are the nodal displacements, $n$ is the number of nodes per element, and $\mathbf{d}$ is the nodal displacement vector.

The **order of the polynomial** chosen dictates the number of nodes in the element:
- **Linear element:** 1st-order polynomial → **2 nodes**
- **Quadratic element:** 2nd-order polynomial → **3 nodes**
- **Cubic element:** 3rd-order polynomial → **4 nodes**

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalized Displacement Field Assumption:** In the FEM, we assume that the displacement at any point inside an element can be **approximated** by a simple polynomial in terms of the spatial coordinate. This is a deliberate simplification — exact solutions of PDEs (like beam equations) are usually complicated transcendental functions, whereas polynomials are easy to differentiate and integrate.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **"Connecting Dots with a Rope" Analogy:** Imagine you are given the **height of a road** at two specific points (say, at km-stone 0 and km-stone 5). You cannot measure the road profile at every single millimetre. What do you do? You connect the two known points with a **straight line** (a linear polynomial). At any intermediate point, the height is simply the value of that straight line. The shape functions $N_1(x)$ and $N_2(x)$ are precisely the **weights** that blend the two known nodal values to give the height at any intermediate location.
>
> - At node 1: weight of $u_1 = 1$, weight of $u_2 = 0$ → $N_1 = 1, N_2 = 0$
> - At node 2: weight of $u_1 = 0$, weight of $u_2 = 1$ → $N_1 = 0, N_2 = 1$
> - In between: both weights are fractional, but always add up to **exactly 1**.

This is the famous **Partition of Unity** property — a guarantee that the approximated field never "jumps" or "dips" artificially between nodes.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit (SI) |
|---|---|---|
| $x$ | Spatial coordinate along bar | $\text{m}$ (metre) |
| $L$ | Length of the bar element | $\text{m}$ |
| $u(x)$ | Displacement at point $x$ | $\text{m}$ |
| $u_1, u_2$ | Nodal displacements at nodes 1 and 2 | $\text{m}$ |
| $E$ | Young's Modulus of bar material | $\text{Pa} = \text{N/m}^2$ |
| $A$ | Cross-sectional area of bar | $\text{m}^2$ |
| $K$ | Element stiffness | $\text{N/m}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (PEMET634, Module 2), the derivation of shape functions for a **one-dimensional bar element using a polynomial of appropriate order** is a fundamental building block. Students must be able to derive shape functions for a **2-noded linear bar** and understand their **properties** in order to construct the **stiffness matrix** in subsequent modules.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Foundation: Why Polynomials?

The displacement $u(x)$ inside a 1D bar is approximated by a polynomial because:

1. **Polynomials are easy to differentiate and integrate** — essential for deriving the strain–displacement matrix $\mathbf{B}$ and stiffness matrix $\mathbf{K}$.
2. **Polynomial order can be increased** to capture more complex deformation patterns (linear → quadratic → cubic).
3. **A polynomial with $n$ terms has $n$ arbitrary constants**, which can be uniquely determined using $n$ nodal conditions.

For a **linear (2-noded) bar element**, the assumed displacement field is:

$$u(x) = a_0 + a_1\, x$$

This has **two unknown constants** ($a_0, a_1$), which can be determined by the **two nodal displacement conditions**.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic (Derivation Blueprint)

The derivation of shape functions for a 2-noded linear bar follows a fixed procedure:

- **Step 1:** Assume a polynomial displacement field: $u(x) = a_0 + a_1 x$
- **Step 2:** Impose nodal boundary conditions at $x_1$ and $x_2$.
- **Step 3:** Form a matrix equation $\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{a}$ linking nodal displacements to polynomial coefficients.
- **Step 4:** Invert the coefficient matrix to solve for $\{a\}$ in terms of $\{u\}$.
- **Step 5:** Substitute $\{a\}$ back into $u(x)$ to obtain $u(x) = N_1(x)\,u_1 + N_2(x)\,u_2$.
- **Step 6:** Identify $N_1(x)$ and $N_2(x)$ as the **shape functions**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula sheet** that examiners expect you to reproduce verbatim in the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination).

| # | Formula / Property | Expression | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Displacement field (linear) | $u(x) = a_0 + a_1 x$ | Two constants, two nodes |
| 2 | Nodal condition at node 1 | $u(x_1) = a_0 + a_1 x_1 = u_1$ | Boundary condition |
| 3 | Nodal condition at node 2 | $u(x_2) = a_0 + a_1 x_2 = u_2$ | Boundary condition |
| 4 | Matrix form | $\begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x_1 \\ 1 & x_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \end{Bmatrix}$ | Coefficient matrix $\mathbf{A}$ |
| 5 | Inverse relation | $\begin{Bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \end{Bmatrix} = \dfrac{1}{L} \begin{bmatrix} x_2 & -x_1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix}$ | Where $L = x_2 - x_1$ |
| 6 | Shape function $N_1(x)$ | $N_1(x) = \dfrac{x_2 - x}{x_2 - x_1} = \dfrac{x_2 - x}{L}$ | 1 at node 1, 0 at node 2 |
| 7 | Shape function $N_2(x)$ | $N_2(x) = \dfrac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \dfrac{x - x_1}{L}$ | 0 at node 1, 1 at node 2 |
| 8 | Compact displacement form | $u(x) = \begin{bmatrix} N_1 & N_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix}$ | Vector form |
| 9 | Kronecker Delta Property | $N_i(x_j) = \delta_{ij}$ | $1$ if $i=j$, $0$ otherwise |
| 10 | Partition of Unity | $N_1(x) + N_2(x) = 1$ | Holds for all $x$ in the element |
| 11 | Natural coordinate | $\xi = \dfrac{x - x_1}{L}$, so $x = x_1 + \xi L$ | Maps $x_1 \to \xi=0$, $x_2 \to \xi=1$ |
| 12 | Shape functions in $\xi$ | $N_1(\xi) = 1 - \xi$, $\quad N_2(\xi) = \xi$ | Valid for $\xi \in [0, 1]$ |
| 13 | Shape functions in $\hat{\xi}$ (symmetric) | $N_1 = \dfrac{1-\hat{\xi}}{2}$, $\quad N_2 = \dfrac{1+\hat{\xi}}{2}$ | Valid for $\hat{\xi} \in [-1, 1]$ |

---

## 2.4 Properties of Shape Functions (Mandatory for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **four properties** of shape functions are repeatedly asked in KTU examinations:

1. **Kronecker Delta Property:** $N_i(x_j) = \delta_{ij}$
   - A shape function equals **unity at its own node** and **zero at all other nodes**.
   - Ensures that the displacement field exactly reproduces the prescribed nodal displacements.

2. **Partition of Unity:** $\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(x) = 1$
   - The sum of all shape functions in an element is identically equal to 1.
   - Guarantees that a **rigid-body translation** (constant $u$) is reproduced exactly.

3. **Polynomial Form:** Each $N_i(x)$ is a polynomial of degree $(p-1)$, where $p$ is the order of the assumed displacement field.
   - For linear: $N_i$ are **linear** in $x$.
   - For quadratic: $N_i$ are **quadratic** in $x$.

4. **Linear Independence:** The shape functions $\{N_1, N_2, \ldots, N_n\}$ are linearly independent.
   - No shape function can be written as a combination of the others.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The shape function concept is the **backbone** of every commercial FEM package (ANSYS, ABAQUS, NASTRAN, COMSOL). The same concept generalizes to:
- **2D elements** (triangular CST, quadrilateral Q4, etc.) for plane stress/strain and heat transfer.
- **3D elements** (tetrahedral, hexahedral) for solid mechanics.
- **Beam and plate elements** for structural analysis.
- **Higher-order elements** (p-refinement) for improved accuracy without mesh refinement.

The derivation done here for a 1D bar is the **conceptual prototype** for ALL of these advanced element formulations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem Setup

Consider a **uniform 1D bar element** of length $L$ with:
- **Node 1** located at coordinate $x_1$ (left end)
- **Node 2** located at coordinate $x_2$ (right end)
- Nodal displacements: $u_1$ at node 1, $u_2$ at node 2
- Element length: $L = x_2 - x_1$

> [!NOTE]
> The exact same derivation applies to a bar of any length. For convenience, many textbooks set $x_1 = 0$ and $x_2 = L$ (bar starting at origin). We will keep the derivation general here.

---

## 3.2 Complete Exhaustive Derivation

### Step 1: Assume a Polynomial Displacement Field

For a **linear 2-noded element**, assume the displacement varies linearly along the length:

$$u(x) = a_0 + a_1\, x$$

**Explanation:** A linear polynomial has 2 unknown coefficients ($a_0, a_1$), which matches the 2 nodal degrees of freedom ($u_1, u_2$). This is the principle of **equal number of polynomial coefficients and nodal DOFs**.

---

### Step 2: Apply Boundary Conditions at the Two Nodes

**At node 1** (located at $x = x_1$, displacement $= u_1$):

$$u(x_1) = a_0 + a_1\, x_1 = u_1$$

**At node 2** (located at $x = x_2$, displacement $= u_2$):

$$u(x_2) = a_0 + a_1\, x_2 = u_2$$

**Explanation:** These two equations enforce that the assumed polynomial **passes exactly through the known nodal values** — a fundamental requirement of any interpolation scheme.

---

### Step 3: Arrange into Matrix Form

The two equations can be compactly written in matrix form as:

$$\begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x_1 \\ 1 & x_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \end{Bmatrix}$$

In shorthand: $\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{A}\, \mathbf{a}$

where $\mathbf{A}$ is the **coefficient matrix** (also called the **nodal coordinate matrix**).

---

### Step 4: Invert the Coefficient Matrix

The determinant of $\mathbf{A}$ is:

$$\det(\mathbf{A}) = (1)(x_2) - (1)(x_1) = x_2 - x_1 = L$$

Since $L \neq 0$, the matrix is invertible. The inverse is:

$$\mathbf{A}^{-1} = \frac{1}{L} \begin{bmatrix} x_2 & -x_1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{Bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \end{Bmatrix} = \frac{1}{L} \begin{bmatrix} x_2 & -x_1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

---

### Step 5: Expand the Inverse Relation Explicitly

Computing the matrix–vector product element-by-element:

$$a_0 = \frac{1}{L} \bigl( x_2\, u_1 - x_1\, u_2 \bigr)$$

$$a_1 = \frac{1}{L} \bigl( -u_1 + u_2 \bigr) = \frac{u_2 - u_1}{L}$$

**Verification (sanity check):** If $x_1 = 0$ and $x_2 = L$, then:
- $a_0 = \frac{1}{L} (L\, u_1 - 0 \cdot u_2) = u_1$ ✓ (matches $u(0) = u_1$)
- $a_1 = \frac{u_2 - u_1}{L}$ ✓ (slope of the line)

---

### Step 6: Substitute Back into the Displacement Field

Recall: $u(x) = a_0 + a_1\, x$

Substituting the expressions for $a_0$ and $a_1$:

$$u(x) = \frac{1}{L} \bigl( x_2\, u_1 - x_1\, u_2 \bigr) + \frac{u_2 - u_1}{L}\, x$$

**Step 6a:** Group terms by $u_1$ and $u_2$:

$$u(x) = u_1 \left( \frac{x_2 - x}{L} \right) + u_2 \left( \frac{x - x_1}{L} \right)$$

**Explanation of grouping:**
- Coefficient of $u_1$: $\dfrac{1}{L} \cdot x_2 - \dfrac{1}{L} \cdot x = \dfrac{x_2 - x}{L}$
- Coefficient of $u_2$: $-\dfrac{1}{L} \cdot x_1 + \dfrac{1}{L} \cdot x = \dfrac{x - x_1}{L}$

**Step 6b:** Identify the shape functions:

$$\boxed{N_1(x) = \frac{x_2 - x}{L} = \frac{x_2 - x}{x_2 - x_1}}$$

$$\boxed{N_2(x) = \frac{x - x_1}{L} = \frac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1}}$$

**Final compact form:**

$$u(x) = N_1(x)\, u_1 + N_2(x)\, u_2 = \begin{bmatrix} N_1(x) & N_2(x) \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix (Compact Notation for Examinations)

The entire derivation can be represented as a single matrix chain (highly appreciated by KTU examiners):

$$\mathbf{N}(x) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x \end{bmatrix} \cdot \mathbf{A}^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{L} \begin{bmatrix} x_2 & -x_1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Carrying out the multiplication:

$$\mathbf{N}(x) = \frac{1}{L} \begin{bmatrix} x_2 - x & x - x_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} N_1(x) & N_2(x) \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trick:** Notice the elegant pattern — the polynomial basis $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & x \end{bmatrix}$ multiplied by $\mathbf{A}^{-1}$ **directly yields the shape function row vector** $\begin{bmatrix} N_1 & N_2 \end{bmatrix}$. This is the standard procedure for deriving shape functions of any order element, not just the 2-noded bar.

---

## 3.4 Verification of Shape Function Properties

### Property 1: Kronecker Delta

**At $x = x_1$:**
$$N_1(x_1) = \frac{x_2 - x_1}{L} = \frac{L}{L} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$
$$N_2(x_1) = \frac{x_1 - x_1}{L} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**At $x = x_2$:**
$$N_1(x_2) = \frac{x_2 - x_2}{L} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$
$$N_2(x_2) = \frac{x_2 - x_1}{L} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

### Property 2: Partition of Unity

$$N_1(x) + N_2(x) = \frac{x_2 - x}{L} + \frac{x - x_1}{L} = \frac{x_2 - x_1}{L} = \frac{L}{L} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

### Property 3: Polynomial Form

Both $N_1(x)$ and $N_2(x)$ are **linear polynomials** in $x$ (consistent with the assumed linear displacement field). ✓

---

## 3.5 Natural Coordinate Representation

It is often convenient to use a **non-dimensional local coordinate** $\xi$ instead of the global $x$:

$$\xi = \frac{x - x_1}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = x_1 + \xi L$$

Differential: $dx = L \, d\xi$

Substituting into the shape functions:

$$N_1 = \frac{x_2 - (x_1 + \xi L)}{L} = \frac{L - \xi L}{L} = 1 - \xi$$

$$N_2 = \frac{(x_1 + \xi L) - x_1}{L} = \frac{\xi L}{L} = \xi$$

**Result (canonical form used in all FEM software):**

$$u(\xi) = (1 - \xi)\, u_1 + \xi\, u_2, \quad \xi \in [0, 1]$$

For a **symmetric** element mapped to $\hat{\xi} \in [-1, 1]$, the substitution $\hat{\xi} = 2\xi - 1$ gives:

$$N_1 = \frac{1 - \hat{\xi}}{2}, \quad N_2 = \frac{1 + \hat{\xi}}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use natural coordinates?** They make the **element formulation independent of the actual length and orientation** of the bar. The same shape functions are valid for any 1D element, regardless of whether it is 1 mm or 100 m long. This is essential for building a general-purpose FEM code.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture Flow of the Derivation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["STEP 1: ASSUME POLYNOMIAL FIELD"]:::startNode
    B["STEP 2: APPLY NODAL BCs AT x1 AND x2"]:::processNode
    C["STEP 3: FORM MATRIX EQUATION u = A a"]:::processNode
    D["STEP 4: INVERT A TO GET a = Ainv u"]:::processNode
    E["STEP 5: SUBSTITUTE a INTO u x"]:::processNode
    F["STEP 6: IDENTIFY N1 x AND N2 x"]:::endNode
    G["VERIFY PROPERTIES: KRONECKER + PARTITION OF UNITY"]:::verifyNode

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G

    classDef startNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef processNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E90FF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef endNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef verifyNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Conceptual Map of the 1D Bar Element

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["NODE 1\nx = x1\nu = u1"]:::nodeBox
    B["NODE 2\nx = x2\nu = u2"]:::nodeBox
    C["SHAPE FUNCTION N1 x\nLINEAR\nVALUE 1 at NODE 1\nVALUE 0 at NODE 2"]:::shapeBox
    D["SHAPE FUNCTION N2 x\nLINEAR\nVALUE 0 at NODE 1\nVALUE 1 at NODE 2"]:::shapeBox

    B --- D
    C --- E

    classDef shapeBox fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fieldBox fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---
