<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Define shape functions in FEM. List any two important properties of shape functions for a one-dimensional bar element.**

**Model Answer:**

A **shape function** is a polynomial interpolation function that gives the value of the field variable (e.g., displacement) at any point inside a finite element as a weighted sum of the nodal values of that field variable.

For a 1D linear bar element with two nodes:

$$u(x) = N_1(x)\, u_1 + N_2(x)\, u_2$$

**Two key properties:**

1. **Kronecker Delta Property:** $N_i(x_j) = \delta_{ij}$, i.e., $N_1(x_1) = 1$, $N_1(x_2) = 0$, $N_2(x_1) = 0$, $N_2(x_2) = 1$.

2. **Partition of Unity:** $N_1(x) + N_2(x) = 1$ for all $x$ in the element, ensuring rigid-body translation is reproduced exactly.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**For a 2-noded 1D bar element of length $L$ with one end at the origin, write the expressions for the shape functions in terms of the local coordinate $\xi \in [0, 1]$. Verify that they satisfy the partition of unity.**

**Model Answer:**

For a 2-noded 1D bar element with $x_1 = 0$ and $x_2 = L$, the shape functions in local coordinate $\xi = x/L$ are:

$$N_1(\xi) = 1 - \xi$$

$$N_2(\xi) = \xi$$

**Verification of Partition of Unity:**

$$N_1 + N_2 = (1 - \xi) + \xi = 1 \quad \forall\, \xi \in [0,1] \quad \checkmark$$

**At the nodes:**
- $\xi = 0$: $N_1 = 1$, $N_2 = 0$ ✓
- $\xi = 1$: $N_1 = 0$, $N_2 = 1$ ✓

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2023]

**For a one-dimensional bar element of length $L$ with nodes at $x_1 = 0$ and $x_2 = L$:**
**(a) Derive the shape functions $N_1(x)$ and $N_2(x)$ by assuming a linear displacement polynomial. (7 Marks)**
**(b) State and prove the properties of the shape functions thus obtained. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Assumed displacement field:**

$$u(x) = a_0 + a_1 x$$

**Apply nodal conditions:**

At node 1 ($x = 0$): $u_1 = a_0 + a_1 (0) = a_0$, so $a_0 = u_1$.

At node 2 ($x = L$): $u_2 = a_0 + a_1 L$, so $a_1 = \dfrac{u_2 - u_1}{L}$.

**Substitute back:**

$$u(x) = u_1 + \frac{u_2 - u_1}{L}\, x = u_1 \left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right) + u_2 \left(\frac{x}{L}\right)$$

**Shape functions:**

$$N_1(x) = 1 - \frac{x}{L} = \frac{L - x}{L}$$

$$N_2(x) = \frac{x}{L}$$

In matrix form:

$$u(x) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{L - x}{L} & \dfrac{x}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Property 1 — Kronecker Delta Property:**

$$N_1(0) = \frac{L - 0}{L} = 1, \quad N_1(L) = \frac{L - L}{L} = 0$$

$$N_2(0) = \frac{0}{L} = 0, \quad N_2(L) = \frac{L}{L} = 1$$

Hence, $N_i(x_j) = \delta_{ij}$. ✓

**Property 2 — Partition of Unity:**

$$N_1(x) + N_2(x) = \frac{L - x}{L} + \frac{x}{L} = \frac{L}{L} = 1 \quad \forall\, x$$

This means a uniform translation $u = c$ (with $u_1 = u_2 = c$) is recovered exactly. ✓

**Property 3 — Polynomial Form:**

Both $N_1$ and $N_2$ are **linear polynomials** in $x$, consistent with the assumed linear displacement field. ✓

**Property 4 — Linear Independence:**

$N_1 \neq c \cdot N_2$ for any constant $c$, so the two functions are linearly independent. ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**For a one-dimensional bar element of length $L$ with nodes at $x_1$ and $x_2$:**
**(a) Derive the shape functions in terms of the global coordinate $x$ using the matrix inversion method. (7 Marks)**
**(b) Express the shape functions in the natural coordinate $\xi \in [0, 1]$ and compute the strain–displacement matrix $\mathbf{B}$. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Assume the displacement field:**

$$u(x) = a_0 + a_1 x$$

**Apply nodal conditions:**

$$u_1 = a_0 + a_1 x_1, \quad u_2 = a_0 + a_1 x_2$$

**Matrix form:**

$$\begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x_1 \\ 1 & x_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Invert the coefficient matrix** (determinant $= x_2 - x_1 = L$):

$$\begin{Bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \end{Bmatrix} = \frac{1}{L} \begin{bmatrix} x_2 & -x_1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Substitute into $u(x) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \end{Bmatrix}$:**

$$u(x) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{L} \begin{bmatrix} x_2 & -x_1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

$$u(x) = \frac{1}{L} \begin{bmatrix} x_2 - x & x - x_1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Therefore:**

$$N_1(x) = \frac{x_2 - x}{L}, \quad N_2(x) = \frac{x - x_1}{L}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Natural coordinate mapping:** $\xi = \dfrac{x - x_1}{L}$, so $x = x_1 + \xi L$.

**Shape functions in $\xi$:**

$$N_1(\xi) = 1 - \xi, \quad N_2(\xi) = \xi$$

**Strain–displacement matrix $\mathbf{B}$:**

Strain $\varepsilon(x) = \dfrac{du}{dx}$.

Using the chain rule with $dx = L\, d\xi$, we have $\dfrac{d}{dx} = \dfrac{1}{L}\dfrac{d}{d\xi}$.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{du}{dx} = \frac{1}{L}\left( \frac{dN_1}{d\xi}\, u_1 + \frac{dN_2}{d\xi}\, u_2 \right)$$

$$\frac{dN_1}{d\xi} = -1, \quad \frac{dN_2}{d\xi} = 1$$

**Therefore:**

$$\mathbf{B} = \frac{1}{L} \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

This is a **constant matrix** (no $x$ or $\xi$ dependence), which is one of the key simplifying features of the 2-noded linear bar element.

> [!NOTE]
> **Follow-up (not required, but useful):** The element stiffness matrix is $\mathbf{K} = \int_0^L \mathbf{B}^T E A\, \mathbf{B}\, dx = \dfrac{EA}{L} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Sign Errors in the Coefficient Matrix Inverse:** Forgetting the negative sign in $\mathbf{A}^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{L}\begin{bmatrix} x_2 & -x_1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ is the **#1 error**. Always verify by computing $\mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{A}^{-1} = \mathbf{I}$.
>
> 2. **Confusion between $N_1$ and $N_2$:** Students often swap the order. **Memory aid:** $N_1$ is associated with **node 1** and equals **1 at $x_1$**, so it must be the expression that yields 1 when $x = x_1$.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to verify the properties:** KTU examiners **always allocate 2–3 marks** for verifying the Kronecker delta and partition of unity. Skipping this is a guaranteed loss.
>
> 4. **Confusing natural coordinates $\xi \in [0,1]$ with symmetric coordinates $\hat{\xi} \in [-1, 1]$:** The formulas $N_1 = 1 - \xi$ and $N_1 = (1-\hat{\xi})/2$ are **different representations**, not the same. State clearly which domain your $\xi$ belongs to.
>
> 5. **Not expressing final answer in matrix form:** The compact form $u(x) = \begin{bmatrix} N_1 & N_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix}$ is worth **dedicated marks** in KTU papers. Don't skip it.
>
> 6. **Mislabeling the displacement as $u$ vs $v$ vs $w$:** For a 1D bar, use $u$ (along $x$). Using $v$ in a 1D context is a dead giveaway that you don't know your basic notation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Derivation of Shape Functions for 1D Bar**

- **Starting point:** Assume $u(x) = a_0 + a_1 x$ for a linear 2-noded bar.
- **Number of unknowns = Number of nodes** (a fundamental FEM principle).
- **Final result for shape functions** (memorize these two expressions):
  - $N_1(x) = \dfrac{x_2 - x}{L}$
  - $N_2(x) = \dfrac{x - x_1}{L}$
- **Natural coordinate form** (used in all FEM software):
  - $N_1(\xi) = 1 - \xi$, $N_2(\xi) = \xi$ for $\xi \in [0,1]$
  - $N_1 = \dfrac{1 - \hat{\xi}}{2}$, $N_2 = \dfrac{1 + \hat{\xi}}{2}$ for $\hat{\xi} \in [-1, 1]$
- **Four key properties** (always verify in your answer):
  1. Kronecker delta: $N_i(x_j) = \delta_{ij}$
  2. Partition of unity: $\sum N_i = 1$
  3. Polynomial form
  4. Linear independence
- **Matrix derivation shortcut:** $\mathbf{N}(x) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{A}^{-1}$
- **Strain–displacement matrix** (frequently asked as follow-up): $\mathbf{B} = \dfrac{1}{L}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
- **Compact notation:** $u(x) = \mathbf{N}(x)\, \mathbf{d}$, where $\mathbf{d} = \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix}$
- **Always state the domain** of $x$ or $\xi$ explicitly: $x \in [x_1, x_2]$, $\xi \in [0,1]$ or $\hat{\xi} \in [-1,1]$.
- **For higher-order elements** (quadratic, cubic), the same matrix-inversion procedure applies with longer polynomial bases: $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & x & x^2 \end{bmatrix}$ for quadratic, etc.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 2-Dimensional CST and 1-Dimensional Beam Element; Convergence Requirement of Shape Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET634 - FINITE ELEMENT METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** 2-Dimensional CST and 1-Dimensional Beam Element; Convergence Requirement of Shape Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Constant Strain Triangle (CST) Element

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> The **Constant Strain Triangle (CST)** is the simplest 2-Dimensional finite element used for plane stress and plane strain analysis. It is a three-noded triangular element (nodes *i*, *j*, *k*) with **two translational degrees of freedom (DOF)** per node — horizontal displacement *u* and vertical displacement *v*. Because the displacement field is assumed linear in both *x* and *y*, the strain components (εx, εy, γxy) are **constant throughout the element**, hence the name "Constant Strain Triangle."

### Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you have a triangular slice of pizza dough. If you hold two corners fixed and stretch the third corner slightly, the dough deforms uniformly across its entire area — every fibre stretches by the same amount. There are no "hot spots" or regions of high strain. This is exactly what the CST element assumes: strain is uniform (constant) over the whole triangle. This makes the CST computationally cheap but only approximate for curved geometries — just as you would need many small triangular pieces to approximate a curved dome.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The area of the triangle is **A = ½ *xᵢ*(yⱼ − yₖ) + xⱼ*(yₖ − yᵢ) + xₖ*(yᵢ − yⱼ)***, computed once and treated as a **constant geometric property** during stiffness formulation.

---

## 1.2 The 1-Dimensional Beam Element

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> The **1-D Beam Element** is a two-noded line element (nodes *i*, *j*) used for Euler-Bernoulli beam bending analysis. Each node carries **two degrees of freedom**: transverse deflection *w* and rotation *θ = dw/dx*. Total DOF per element = **4**. The element shape functions are **Hermite cubic polynomials** of degree 3, ensuring that the deflection *w* and slope *dw/dx* are continuous (C¹ continuity) across element boundaries.

### Conceptual Analogy

Think of a soft ruler held between two hands. If you tilt the left hand slightly and lift the right hand up, the ruler curves smoothly into a gentle S-shape. The deflection is smooth (no kinks), and the slope changes continuously — there are no sudden jumps in angle at the joints. The Hermite cubic shape functions for the beam element enforce exactly this smooth, kinking-free behaviour. Unlike a bar (which only stretches), a beam resists **bending** through its flexural rigidity **EI**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics:** Flexural Rigidity = **EI** (N·m²), where **E** is Young's Modulus and **I** is the Second Moment of Area.

---

## 1.3 Convergence Requirement of Shape Functions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Convergence** in the FEM context means that as the mesh is refined (element size → 0), the finite element solution approaches the exact solution of the governing differential equation. For convergence to be guaranteed, the assumed shape functions must satisfy two fundamental conditions:
> 1. **Completeness Criterion** — ability to represent rigid body motion and constant strain states exactly.
> 2. **Compatibility Criterion** — displacement continuity across element boundaries (no overlaps, no gaps).

### Conceptual Analogy

Suppose you are tiling a bathroom floor with small ceramic triangles. For the floor to look smooth and be waterproof:
1. The tiles must be able to lie perfectly flat (representing "no deformation") and tilt uniformly (representing "constant strain").
2. The boundaries of adjacent tiles must match perfectly — no overlap, no gap.

The same two requirements govern FEM shape functions. If they fail, the numerical solution will either be too stiff, too soft, or oscillate wildly as the mesh is refined.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 CST Element — Theoretical Framework

### Step-by-Step Construction Logic

1. **Nodal DOF vector** for a triangle: 
   $\{d\}^e = \{u_i, v_i, u_j, v_j, u_k, v_k\}^T$ — a **6×1** column.

2. **Assumed displacement field** (linear in *x*, *y*):
   $u(x,y) = \alpha_1 + \alpha_2 x + \alpha_3 y$
   $v(x,y) = \alpha_4 + \alpha_5 x + \alpha_6 y$

3. **Solving for coefficients** at the three nodal coordinates yields shape functions:
   $N_i(x,y) = \dfrac{a_i + b_i x + c_i y}{2A}$

4. **Geometric coefficients** for node *i*:
   $a_i = x_j y_k - x_k y_j$
   $b_i = y_j - y_k$
   $c_i = x_k - x_j$

5. **Strain–Displacement (B-matrix)** is constant:
   $[B] = \dfrac{1}{2A}\begin{bmatrix} b_i & 0 & b_j & 0 & b_k & 0 \\ 0 & c_i & 0 & c_j & 0 & c_k \\ c_i & b_i & c_j & b_j & c_k & b_k \end{bmatrix}$

6. **Element stiffness matrix:**
   $[k]^e = \int_A [B]^T [D] [B] \, t \, dA = A \cdot t \cdot [B]^T [D] [B]$
   (since **B** is constant, integration over area reduces to multiplication by **A**).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** CST is the workhorse of commercial packages (ANSYS, ABAQUS) for preliminary 2-D stress analysis of dams, plates, and soil mechanics problems where mesh adaptation is needed.

---

## 2.2 Beam Element — Theoretical Framework

### Step-by-Step Construction Logic

1. **Nodal DOF vector:**
   $\{d\}^e = \{w_i, \theta_i, w_j, \theta_j\}^T$ — a **4×1** column.

2. **Assumed displacement field** (Hermite cubic):
   $w(x) = N_1 w_i + N_2 \theta_i + N_3 w_j + N_4 \theta_j$

3. **Hermite Shape Functions** (with $\xi = x/L$):
   $N_1 = 1 - 3\xi^2 + 2\xi^3$
   $N_2 = L(\xi - 2\xi^2 + \xi^3)$
   $N_3 = 3\xi^2 - 2\xi^3$
   $N_4 = L(-\xi^2 + \xi^3)$

4. **Curvature–Displacement relationship:**
   $\dfrac{d^2 w}{dx^2} = [B]\{d\}^e$

5. **Element stiffness matrix:**
   $[k]^e = \int_0^L EI \left(\dfrac{d^2 N}{dx^2}\right)^T \left(\dfrac{d^2 N}{dx^2}\right) dx$

6. **Final closed-form** expression (the famous 4×4 beam matrix).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** Beam elements model bridge girders, machine tool frames, aircraft wings, and building columns. The Hermite formulation is essential because beam stiffness depends on **second derivatives** of deflection (curvature → bending moment).

---

## 2.3 Convergence Criteria — Theoretical Framework

### Why the Two Criteria?

- **Completeness** ensures that *constant* and *linear* fields of strain can be captured *exactly*. If the shape functions cannot represent a rigid body translation, the assembled structure will resist its own rigid motion — producing **spurious stresses**.
- **Compatibility** ensures no kinematic discontinuities between adjacent elements. A discontinuity in deflection would mean two elements physically overlap or separate — physically impossible for a continuous body.

### Mathematical Statement

A shape function set $N_i$ is **convergent** if and only if:
1. $\sum_i N_i(x,y) = 1$ everywhere (partition of unity — guarantees rigid body translation).
2. $\sum_i x \cdot N_i(x,y) = x$ and $\sum_i y \cdot N_i(x,y) = y$ (guarantees rigid body rotation).
3. $\sum_i N_i(x,y)$ is a **linear function** of *x* and *y* in global coordinates (guarantees constant strain).

These three statements together form the **Patch Test** (Irons, 1965).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Element / Concept | Key Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CST Area | $A = \tfrac{1}{2}(x_i(y_j - y_k) + x_j(y_k - y_i) + x_k(y_i - y_j))$ | m² |
| 2 | CST Shape Function | $N_i = (a_i + b_i x + c_i y)/(2A)$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | CST Geometric Coeff. | $b_i = y_j - y_k$, $c_i = x_k - x_j$ | Length (m) |
| 4 | CST Strain Matrix | $[B]$ is constant, dimension 3×6 | — |
| 5 | CST Stiffness | $[k]^e = A\,t\,[B]^T[D][B]$ | N/m |
| 6 | Plane Stress [D] | $\dfrac{E}{1-\nu^2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & (1-\nu)/2 \end{bmatrix}$ | N/m² |
| 7 | Plane Strain [D] | $\dfrac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}\begin{bmatrix} 1-\nu & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1-\nu & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & (1-2\nu)/2 \end{bmatrix}$ | N/m² |
| 8 | Beam Shape $N_1$ | $1 - 3\xi^2 + 2\xi^3$ | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Beam Shape $N_2$ | $L(\xi - 2\xi^2 + \xi^3)$ | m |
| 10 | Beam Shape $N_3$ | $3\xi^2 - 2\xi^3$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Beam Shape $N_4$ | $L(-\xi^2 + \xi^3)$ | m |
| 12 | Beam [B] | $\tfrac{1}{L^2}[6 - 12\xi,\; L(4 - 6\xi),\; -6 + 12\xi,\; L(-2 + 6\xi)]$ | 1/m² |
| 13 | Beam [k] coefficient | $\tfrac{EI}{L^3}$ (matrix) | N/m |
| 14 | Beam Entry (1,1) | $12 EI/L^3$ | N/m |
| 15 | Beam Entry (1,2) | $6 EI/L^2$ | N |
| 16 | Beam Entry (2,2) | $4 EI/L$ | N·m |
| 17 | Convergence Cond. 1 | $\sum N_i = 1$ | Partition of unity |
| 18 | Convergence Cond. 2 | $\sum x_i N_i = x$ | Rigid body rotn. |
| 19 | Convergence Cond. 3 | $C^0$ continuity across edges | No gaps/overlaps |
| 20 | Beam continuity | $C^1$ continuity (w and dw/dx) | Hermite req. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 CST Shape Function Derivation (Exhaustive)

**Step 1 — Assume displacement field linear in *x*, *y*:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u(x,y) &= \alpha_1 + \alpha_2 x + \alpha_3 y \\
v(x,y) &= \alpha_4 + \alpha_5 x + \alpha_6 y
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Impose nodal conditions at (xᵢ, yᵢ), (xⱼ, yⱼ), (xₖ, yₖ):**

For *u* at node *i*:
$$u_i = \alpha_1 + \alpha_2 x_i + \alpha_3 y_i$$

Similarly:
$$u_j = \alpha_1 + \alpha_2 x_j + \alpha_3 y_k$$
$$u_k = \alpha_1 + \alpha_2 x_k + \alpha_3 y_k$$

**Step 3 — Solve the 3×3 linear system for α₁, α₂, α₃ using Cramer's rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_1 &= \tfrac{1}{2A}(u_i(x_j y_k - x_k y_j) + u_j(x_k y_i - x_i y_k) + u_k(x_i y_j - x_j y_i)) \\
\alpha_2 &= \tfrac{1}{2A}(u_i(y_j - y_k) + u_j(y_k - y_i) + u_k(y_i - y_j)) \\
\alpha_3 &= \tfrac{1}{2A}(u_i(x_k - x_j) + u_j(x_i - x_k) + u_k(x_j - x_i))
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into u(x,y) and group by nodal DOFs:**

$$
u(x,y) = u_i \cdot \underbrace{\tfrac{a_i + b_i x + c_i y}{2A}}_{N_i} + u_j \cdot \underbrace{\tfrac{a_j + b_j x + c_j y}{2A}}_{N_j} + u_k \cdot \underbrace{\tfrac{a_k + b_k x + c_k y}{2A}}_{N_k}
$$

Where (for node *i*):
$$
a_i = x_j y_k - x_k y_j, \qquad b_i = y_j - y_k, \qquad c_i = x_k - x_j
$$

**Step 5 — Similarly derive v(x,y)** with v_i, v_j, v_k replacing u_i, u_j, u_k.

---

## 3.2 CST Strain Matrix [B] Derivation

The strain vector in 2-D plane problems is:

$$
\{\varepsilon\} = \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \\ \gamma_{xy} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \partial/\partial x & 0 \\ 0 & \partial/\partial y \\ \partial/\partial y & \partial/\partial x \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u \\ v \end{Bmatrix}
$$

Apply partial derivatives to the shape functions. Since $N_i = (a_i + b_i x + c_i y)/(2A)$:

$$
\frac{\partial N_i}{\partial x} = \frac{b_i}{2A}, \qquad \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial y} = \frac{c_i}{2A}
$$

Therefore the strain–displacement matrix is:

$$
[B] = \frac{1}{2A}\begin{bmatrix} b_i & 0 & b_j & 0 & b_k & 0 \\ 0 & c_i & 0 & c_j & 0 & c_k \\ c_i & b_i & c_j & b_j & c_k & b_k \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Because every term in **[B]** depends only on **nodal coordinates** (which are constant), **[B]** is **constant** for a given CST element. This is why the strain throughout the triangle is constant.

---

## 3.3 CST Stiffness Matrix Derivation

Using the principle of minimum potential energy, the stiffness matrix is:

$$
[k]^e = \int_A [B]^T [D] [B] \, t \, dA
$$

Since **[B]** is constant and **t** is the uniform thickness:

$$
[k]^e = A \cdot t \cdot [B]^T [D] [B]
$$

This is a **6×6 symmetric** matrix. Explicitly written for clarity:

$$
[k]^e = \frac{A \cdot t}{4A^2} \, [B_{\text{coeff}}]^T [D] [B_{\text{coeff}}] = \frac{t}{4A} [B_{\text{coeff}}]^T [D] [B_{\text{coeff}}]
$$

where $[B_{\text{coeff}}]$ is the **2A·B** matrix.

---

## 3.4 Beam Element Hermite Shape Function Derivation

**Step 1 — Assume a cubic polynomial:**
$$w(x) = a + bx + cx^2 + dx^3$$

**Step 2 — Impose 4 boundary conditions** at x = 0 and x = L:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w(0) = w_i \;&\Rightarrow\; a = w_i \\
w'(0) = \theta_i \;&\Rightarrow\; b = \theta_i \\
w(L) = w_j \;&\Rightarrow\; a + bL + cL^2 + dL^3 = w_j \\
w'(L) = \theta_j \;&\Rightarrow\; b + 2cL + 3dL^2 = \theta_j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the system for a, b, c, d.**

Substitute $a = w_i$ and $b = \theta_i$ into the third and fourth equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
cL^2 + dL^3 &= w_j - w_i - \theta_i L \\
2cL + 3dL^2 &= \theta_j - \theta_i
\end{aligned}
$$

Divide first by L² and second by L, and set $\xi = x/L$:

Solving the resulting 2×2 system:

$$
c = \frac{1}{L^2}\bigl(-3w_i - 2\theta_i L + 3w_j - \theta_j L\bigr)
$$

$$
d = \frac{1}{L^3}\bigl(2w_i + \theta_i L - 2w_j + \theta_j L\bigr)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into $w(x)$ and regroup by nodal DOFs:**

$$
w(x) = N_1 w_i + N_2 \theta_i + N_3 w_j + N_4 \theta_j
$$

With $\xi = x/L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_1(\xi) &= 1 - 3\xi^2 + 2\xi^3 \\
N_2(\xi) &= L(\xi - 2\xi^2 + \xi^3) \\
N_3(\xi) &= 3\xi^2 - 2\xi^3 \\
N_4(\xi) &= L(-\xi^2 + \xi^3)
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Beam Element [B] Matrix Derivation

Bending curvature relation:

$$
\kappa = -\frac{d^2 w}{dx^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d^2 w}{dx^2} = [B]\{d\}^e
$$

Differentiate $N_2$ and $N_4$ twice with respect to *x* (the $N_1$ and $N_3$ second derivatives cancel the *w* terms but contribute to [B]):

$$
\frac{d^2 N_1}{dx^2} = \frac{1}{L^2}(-6 + 12\xi)
$$

$$
\frac{d^2 N_2}{dx^2} = \frac{1}{L}(-4 + 6\xi)
$$

$$
\frac{d^2 N_3}{dx^2} = \frac{1}{L^2}(6 - 12\xi)
$$

$$
\frac{d^2 N_4}{dx^2} = \frac{1}{L}(-2 + 6\xi)
$$

Therefore:

$$
[B] = \frac{1}{L^2}\begin{bmatrix} (6 - 12\xi) & L(4 - 6\xi) & (-6 + 12\xi) & L(-2 + 6\xi) \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

## 3.6 Beam Element Stiffness Matrix (Final Closed Form)

Integrate over element length:

$$
[k]^e = \int_0^L EI [B]^T [B] \, dx = \frac{EI}{L^3}\begin{bmatrix} 12 & 6L & -12 & 6L \\ 6L & 4L^2 & -6L & 2L^2 \\ -12 & -6L & 12 & -6L \\ 6L & 2L^2 & -6L & 4L^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symmetry Check:** $[k]^e$ is symmetric ($k_{ij} = k_{ji}$) ✓
> **Singularity Check:** $\det[k]^e = 0$ — three rigid body modes (1 translation + 2 rotations in 2-D plane) ✓

---

## 3.7 Convergence Criterion Verification for CST

**Test 1 — Partition of Unity:**
$$\sum_{m=1}^{3} N_m = \tfrac{1}{2A}\bigl[(a_i+a_j+a_k) + (b_i+b_j+b_k)x + (c_i+c_j+c_k)y\bigr]$$

The coefficient of *x* is $(b_i+b_j+b_k) = (y_j-y_k)+(y_k-y_i)+(y_i-y_j) = 0$. Similarly for *y*. The constant term is $(a_i+a_j+a_k) = 2A$. Hence the sum equals **1** ✓.

**Test 2 — Linear Reproduction:** $\sum x_m N_m = x$, $\sum y_m N_m = y$ — easily verified using the same identities.

> [!NOTE]
> CST therefore passes the completeness test exactly and is **C⁰-continuous** along edges (compatibility) — hence **convergent**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CST Element Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Nodal Inputs: u_i v_i u_j v_j u_k v_k] --> B[Shape Function Matrix N]
    A --> C[Geometric Coefficients a_i b_i c_i]
    C --> D[B-Matrix Formulation]
    B --> E[Displacement Field u x y v x y]
    D --> F[Constant Strain Tensor]
    F --> G[Stress via Constitutive D Matrix]
    G --> H[Element Stiffness k e equals A times t times B transpose D B]
    H --> I[Global Assembly]
    I --> J[Apply BCs and Solve]
```

**Caption:** This block architecture shows how the six nodal degrees of freedom of a CST element are combined with constant geometric and material data to produce the 6×6 element stiffness matrix ready for global assembly.

---

## 4.2 Beam Element Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Hermite N1] --> K[Stiffness Matrix k e]
    N2[Hermite N2] --> K
    N3[Hermite N3] --> K
    N4[Hermite N4] --> K
    EI[Flexural Rigidity EI] --> K
    L[Element Length L] --> K
    K --> M1[DOF 1: w_i]
    K --> M2[DOF 2: theta_i]
    K --> M3[DOF 3: w_j]
    K --> M4[DOF 4: theta_j]
```

**Caption:** Each of the four Hermite shape functions, scaled by the flexural rigidity EI and length L, contributes a column of the 4×4 beam element stiffness matrix. The matrix directly couples deflection and rotation DOFs at the two end nodes.

---

## 4.3 Convergence Criterion Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Choose Shape Functions] --> Q1{Partition of Unity?}
    Q1 -- No --> F1[FAIL: Add constant term]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Linear reproduction?}
    Q2 -- No --> F2[FAIL: Include linear terms]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Compatible across edges?}
    Q3 -- No --> F3[FAIL: Increase polynomial order]
    Q3 -- Yes --> P[PATCH TEST PASSED]
    P --> R[Solution converges to exact as h approaches 0]
```

**Caption:** This three-step test (Irons' Patch Test) is the universally accepted minimum check for any new finite element. CST and Beam elements both pass all three gates.

---

## 4.4 Beam Element Physical Schematic (Text-Based Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LeftEnd[Left Node i]
        Wi[Deflection w_i]
        Ti[Rotation theta_i]
    end
    subgraph Element[Beam of Length L and Rigidity EI]
        Curve[Hermite Cubic Shape]
    end
    subgraph RightEnd[Right Node j]
        Wj[Deflection w_j]
        Tj[Rotation theta_j]
    end
    Wi --> Curve
    Ti --> Curve
    Wj --> Curve
    Tj --> Curve
    Curve --> Stiff[4x4 Stiffness Matrix coupling all DOFs]
```

**Caption:** Each nodal DOF (deflection *w* and rotation *θ*) independently drives the Hermite cubic shape, and the resulting curvature energy integrates to form the symmetric 4×4 stiffness matrix.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**State the convergence requirements for shape functions in the finite element method.**

**Model Answer:**

The convergence requirements for shape functions are:

1. **Completeness Criterion:** The shape functions must be able to represent:
   - Rigid body translations (constant displacement field).
   - Rigid body rotations (linear displacement field).
   - Constant strain states.

   Mathematically: $\sum_i N_i = 1$ and $\sum_i x_i N_i = x$, $\sum_i y_i N_i = y$.

2. **Compatibility Criterion:** The displacement field must be continuous across element boundaries. For C⁰ elements (e.g., CST) only displacement continuity is needed; for C¹ elements (e.g., beam) both displacement and slope continuity is required.

> **Marks Distribution:** (2 marks for the two criteria with definitions, 1 mark for the mathematical statement).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Why is the strain constant throughout a CST element?**

**Model Answer:**

In a CST element, the assumed displacement field is linear in both *x* and *y*:

$$u(x,y) = \alpha_1 + \alpha_2 x + \alpha_3 y, \quad v(x,y) = \alpha_4 + \alpha_5 x + \alpha_6 y$$

The strain components are the first partial derivatives:

$$\varepsilon_x = \partial u/\partial x = \alpha_2, \quad \varepsilon_y = \partial v/\partial y = \alpha_6, \quad \gamma_{xy} = \partial u/\partial y + \partial v/\partial x = \alpha_3 + \alpha_5$$

Since the derivatives of linear functions are **constants** independent of position, the entire strain tensor is constant throughout the element — hence the name "Constant Strain Triangle."

> **Marks Distribution:** (1.5 marks for derivation, 1 mark for the conclusion, 0.5 mark for mentioning the name origin).

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A (a)** Derive the shape functions $N_1, N_2, N_3, N_4$ for a 1-D beam element of length *L* using Hermite cubic polynomials. **(7 marks)**

**A (b)** A simply supported beam of length *L* = 4 m carries a uniformly distributed load *w* = 10 kN/m. Using a single beam element with EI = 30,000 kN·m², determine the mid-span deflection and end rotations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a) — Hermite Shape Function Derivation

**Step 1 — Assume a cubic polynomial:**

$$w(x) = a + bx + cx^2 + dx^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions:**

At x = 0: $w(0) = w_i \Rightarrow a = w_i$; $w'(0) = \theta_i \Rightarrow b = \theta_i$.

At x = L: $w(L) = w_j$ and $w'(L) = \theta_j$.

**Step 3 — Write the two remaining equations** with $a$ and $b$ substituted:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_i + \theta_i L + cL^2 + dL^3 &= w_j \\
\theta_i + 2cL + 3dL^2 &= \theta_j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for c and d:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c &= \frac{1}{L^2}\bigl(-3w_i - 2\theta_i L + 3w_j - \theta_j L\bigr) \\
d &= \frac{1}{L^3}\bigl(2w_i + \theta_i L - 2w_j + \theta_j L\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange in shape function form** $w(x) = N_1 w_i + N_2 \theta_i + N_3 w_j + N_4 \theta_j$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_1(x) &= 1 - 3\xi^2 + 2\xi^3 \\
N_2(x) &= L(\xi - 2\xi^2 + \xi^3) \\
N_3(x) &= 3\xi^2 - 2\xi^3 \\
N_4(x) &= L(-\xi^2 + \xi^3)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\xi = x/L$.

**Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_1(0) = 1, &\quad N_1'(0) = 0, \quad N_1(L) = 0, \quad N_1'(L) = 0 \\
N_2(0) = 0, &\quad N_2'(0) = 1, \quad N_2(L) = 0, \quad N_2'(L) = 0 \\
N_3(0) = 0, &\quad N_3'(0) = 0, \quad N_3(L) = 1, \quad N_3'(L) = 0 \\
N_4(0) = 0, &\quad N_4'(0) = 0, \quad N_4(L) = 0, \quad N_4'(L) = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

All Kronecker-delta properties are satisfied ✓.

---

#### Solution to A(b) — Single Beam Element Analysis

**Given:** L = 4 m, w = 10 kN/m, EI = 30,000 kN·m².

**Step 1 — Equivalent nodal load vector** for UDL on a beam element (from consistent load formulation):

$$
\{F\}^e = \int_0^L w \cdot \{N\}^T dx = \frac{wL}{2}\begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ L/6 \\ 1 \\ -L/6 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\{F\}^e = \frac{10 \times 4}{2}\begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 4/6 \\ 1 \\ -4/6 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 20 \\ 13.33 \\ 20 \\ -13.33 \end{Bmatrix} \text{ kN and kN·m}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions** for simply supported beam:

- At node 1 (x=0): $w_1 = 0$ → eliminate row/col 1.
- At node 2 (x=L): $w_3 = 0$ → eliminate row/col 3 (using DOF indexing w₁, θ₁, w₂, θ₂).

The reduced stiffness matrix (retaining rotational DOFs only):

$$
[k]_{\text{red}} = \frac{EI}{L^3}\begin{bmatrix} 4L^2 & 2L^2 \\ 2L^2 & 4L^2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{30{,}000}{64}\begin{bmatrix} 64 & 32 \\ 32 & 64 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 30{,}000 & 15{,}000 \\ 15{,}000 & 30{,}000 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve reduced system:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 30{,}000 & 15{,}000 \\ 15{,}000 & 30{,}000 \end{bmatrix}\begin{Bmatrix} \theta_1 \\ \theta_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 13.33 \\ -13.33 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

By symmetry $\theta_2 = -\theta_1$. From the first equation:

$$
30{,}000\,\theta_1 + 15{,}000(-\theta_1) = 13.33 \Rightarrow 15{,}000\,\theta_1 = 13.33
$$

$$
\theta_1 = 8.89 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}, \quad \theta_2 = -8.89 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 4 — Mid-span deflection** (using Hermite interpolation at $\xi = 0.5$):

$$
w(L/2) = N_3(0.5)\cdot 0 + N_4(0.5)\cdot\theta_2 \quad (\text{since } w_1=w_2=0)
$$

$$
N_3(0.5) = 3(0.25) - 2(0.125) = 0.75 - 0.25 = 0.5
$$

$$
N_4(0.5) = 4(-0.25 + 0.125) = 4(-0.125) = -0.5 \text{ m}
$$

$$
w(L/2) = 0.5(0) + (-0.5)(-8.89 \times 10^{-4}) = 4.44 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.444 \text{ mm}
$$

**Exact analytical deflection:** $\delta_{\text{exact}} = 5wL^4/(384\,EI) = 5(10)(256)/(384 \times 30{,}000) = 1.11 \times 10^{-3}$ m = **1.11 mm**

The single-element FEM result (0.444 mm) under-predicts the exact value — this is because **one element cannot capture the cubic curvature exactly**.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**B (a)** For a 2-D CST element with vertices i(0, 0), j(4, 0), k(0, 3), thickness t = 10 mm, Young's modulus E = 200 GPa, Poisson's ratio ν = 0.3, determine the geometric coefficients and form the [B] matrix for plane stress. **(7 marks)**

**B (b)** Discuss the convergence requirements of shape functions in detail, with reference to the patch test. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a) — CST [B] Matrix Calculation

**Step 1 — Compute geometric coefficients:**

Given nodes: $i(0,0)$, $j(4,0)$, $k(0,3)$.

**For node i:**
$$a_i = x_j y_k - x_k y_j = 4(3) - 0(0) = 12$$
$$b_i = y_j - y_k = 0 - 3 = -3$$
$$c_i = x_k - x_j = 0 - 4 = -4$$

**For node j:**
$$a_j = x_k y_i - x_i y_k = 0(0) - 0(3) = 0$$
$$b_j = y_k - y_i = 3 - 0 = 3$$
$$c_j = x_i - x_k = 0 - 0 = 0$$

**For node k:**
$$a_k = x_i y_j - x_j y_i = 0(0) - 4(0) = 0$$
$$b_k = y_i - y_j = 0 - 0 = 0$$
$$c_k = x_j - x_i = 4 - 0 = 4$$

**Step 2 — Calculate area:**

$$A = \tfrac{1}{2}|x_i(y_j-y_k) + x_j(y_k-y_i) + x_k(y_i-y_j)| = \tfrac{1}{2}|0 + 4(3) + 0| = 6 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Form the [B] matrix:**

$$
[B] = \frac{1}{2(6)}\begin{bmatrix} -3 & 0 & 3 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -4 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 4 \\ -4 & -3 & 0 & 3 & 4 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
[B] = \frac{1}{12}\begin{bmatrix} -3 & 0 & 3 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -4 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 4 \\ -4 & -3 & 0 & 3 & 4 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
[B] = \begin{bmatrix} -0.25 & 0 & 0.25 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -0.333 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0.333 \\ -0.333 & -0.25 & 0 & 0.25 & 0.333 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(units: 1/m)}
$$

**Step 4 — Plane Stress [D] matrix:**

$$
[D] = \frac{200 \times 10^9}{1 - 0.09}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.3 & 0 \\ 0.3 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.35 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 219.78 & 65.93 & 0 \\ 65.93 & 219.78 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 76.92 \end{bmatrix} \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Element stiffness** $[k]^e = At[B]^T[D][B]$ (a 6×6 matrix with values in GN/m).

---

#### Solution to B(b) — Convergence Requirements Detailed Discussion

**The Patch Test (Irons, 1965):**

For an element to be **convergent** (i.e., the FEM solution approaches the exact solution as the mesh is refined), the shape functions must satisfy three patch-test conditions:

1. **Partition of Unity (Constant Displacement Test):**
   $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(x,y) = 1$$
   This guarantees that a uniform rigid-body translation produces zero strain — i.e., the element cannot develop spurious stress from pure rigid motion.

2. **Linear Reproduction (Constant Strain Test):**
   $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i N_i(x,y) = x, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i N_i(x,y) = y$$
   This guarantees that constant strain states (e.g., pure tension, pure shear) are captured exactly by the element. Failure of this test means the element is "too stiff" or "too soft" — leading to poor convergence.

3. **Compatibility (Boundary Continuity Test):**
   Displacements (and for beam/shell, also rotations) must be continuous across inter-element boundaries. Elements meeting this condition are called **conforming** or **compatible elements**.

**Discussion of CST and Beam Elements:**

- **CST (C⁰ element):** Satisfies all three patch-test conditions. It is convergent for plane elasticity problems. ✓
- **Beam element (C¹ element):** Satisfies all three conditions; additionally enforces slope continuity (Hermite property) which is required since beam stiffness depends on second derivatives of displacement. ✓
- **Non-conforming elements** (e.g., some early plate elements) fail condition 3 but may still pass the patch test and converge — these are called **non-conforming but convergent** elements.

> **Real-world significance:** The patch test is the cornerstone of element validation in commercial FE codes. Any new element proposed in research MUST first pass the patch test before being accepted.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign convention errors** in CST geometric coefficients ($b_i, c_i$) — examiners frequently deduct 1–2 marks for inconsistent cyclic ordering. Always verify using the cyclic rule: $b_i = y_j - y_k$ (counter-clockwise positive).
> 2. **For beam elements**, students often forget that $\theta_2 = dw/dx$ is **positive downward** for some conventions — leading to sign flips in the consistent load vector. Always re-derive the [B] matrix explicitly rather than memorizing.
> 3. **Convergence**: Students frequently confuse "compatibility" with "completeness." Remember — completeness is about *polynomial content*, while compatibility is about *boundary continuity*.
> 4. **For CST stiffness**: Because $[B]$ is constant, do NOT perform numerical integration over area — simply multiply by A. Many students lose 2 marks here by integrating unnecessarily.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **CST Element:**
  - 3 nodes, 6 DOF, area A = ½(xᵢ(yⱼ−yₖ)+...).
  - Linear shape functions $N_i = (a_i + b_i x + c_i y)/(2A)$.
  - Strain matrix **[B]** is **constant** — strain is uniform throughout.
  - Stiffness $[k]^e = A t [B]^T [D] [B]$ — no integration needed.
  - Best for coarse meshes; not accurate for stress concentrations.

- **Beam Element:**
  - 2 nodes, 4 DOF (w, θ at each end).
  - Hermite cubic shape functions $N_1$ to $N_4$ with $\xi = x/L$.
  - Stiffness uses $\int EI(d^2N/dx^2)^T(d^2N/dx^2)dx$.
  - Final matrix: 12, 6L, −12, 6L pattern with EI/L³ prefactor.
  - Requires $C^1$ continuity — w AND dw/dx continuous.

- **Convergence Criteria:**
  - **Completeness:** $\sum N_i = 1$ (rigid body translation); linear reproduction (constant strain).
  - **Compatibility:** $C^0$ for CST; $C^1$ for beam.
  - **Patch Test:** Combined check that an element can exactly reproduce constant and linear displacement fields over a patch of elements.
  - **Necessary but not sufficient** — additional tests (e.g., eigenvalue test, isotropy test) exist for advanced elements.

- **Key Constants to Memorize:**
  - Plane Stress [D]: factor $E/(1-\nu^2)$.
  - Plane Strain [D]: factor $E/((1+\nu)(1-2\nu))$.
  - Beam entry (1,1): $12 EI/L^3$.
  - Beam entry (2,2): $4 EI/L$.

- **Common Exam Mistakes:**
  - Mixing up node ordering (i → j → k must be counter-clockwise for positive area).
  - Forgetting the factor *t* (thickness) in CST stiffness.
  - Using wrong [D] matrix (plane stress vs plane strain).
  - Confusing $N_2$ and $N_4$ of beam element — both have factor L but different signs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pascal Triangle; Shape Functions using Lagrange Polynomial
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET634 - FINITE ELEMENT METHODS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Pascal Triangle; Shape Functions using Lagrange Polynomial # 📘 MODULE 2 — PASCAL'S TRIANGLE & SHAPE FUNCTIONS USING LAGRANGE POLYNOMIAL ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Pascal's Triangle — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Pascal's Triangle** is a triangular array of the **binomial coefficients** that arise in binomial expansion. The entry in row $n$ and column $k$ (both zero-indexed) is $\binom{n}{k} = \dfrac{n!}{k!(n-k)!}$, where $n \ge 0$ and $0 \le k \le n$. In Finite Element Method (FEM), Pascal's Triangle provides a **systematic visual pattern** for generating the coefficient matrix used to construct the **monomial basis** of polynomial shape functions for 1-D bar/beam elements. Each row of the triangle corresponds to a polynomial of progressively higher order: $$ P_0(x) = 1 $$ $$ P_1(x) = 1 + x $$ $$ P_2(x) = 1 + 2x + x^2 $$ $$ P_3(x) = 1 + 3x + 3x^2 + x^3 $$ ### 1.2 Shape Functions — Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > A **Shape Function** $N_i(x)$ is a polynomial interpolation function associated with node $i$ of a finite element, such that: > 1. $N_i(x_j) = \delta_{ij}$ (**Kronecker Delta Property** — equals 1 at its own node, 0 at all other nodes). > 2. $\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(x) = 1$ (**Partition of Unity**). > 3. The approximate field variable $u(x) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(x) \, u_i$, where $u_i$ are nodal values. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview 🧠 **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a **stretchy rubber strip** stretched between two thumbtacks on a wall. If you want to know the height (deflection) of the strip at *any* point along its length, you only measure the heights at the two thumbtacks. The shape function acts as a **"mathematical recipe"** telling you how to blend (interpolate) those two measured thumbtack heights to estimate the height at any point in between. For three thumbtacks, the recipe becomes a quadratic (parabolic) curve. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic:** *"Pascal gives the polynomial SKILL, Lagrange gives the polynomial FUNCTION."* Pascal's triangle structures the polynomial form; Lagrange interpolation builds the actual shape functions. ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Natural coordinate range** for a 1-D element: $\xi \in [-1, +1]$ (parent element domain). - **Local coordinate** $x$ ranges over element length $L$. - **Total number of nodes** in an $n^{th}$-order 1-D element: $n + 1$. - **Total degrees of freedom (DOF) per node** in 1-D bar: **1** (axial displacement $u$). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Pascal's Triangle — Construction Logic The Pascal's Triangle for the first five rows is constructed as follows: | Row $n$ | Binomial Expansion | Triangle Row | No. of Elements | |:-------:|:-------------------|:------------:|:---------------:| | **0** | $(1+x)^0$ | $1$ | **1** | | **1** | $(1+x)^1$ | $1 \quad 1$ | **2** | | **2** | $(1+x)^2$ | $1 \quad 2 \quad 1$ | **3** | | **3** | $(1+x)^3$ | $1 \quad 3 \quad 3 \quad 1$ | **4** | | **4** | $(1+x)^4$ | $1 \quad 4 \quad 6 \quad 4 \quad 1$ | **5** | ### 2.2 Construction Rules (Operational Logic Steps) - **Rule 1 — Apex:** Every triangle starts with a single "1" at the apex (row 0). - **Rule 2 — Boundary edges:** The first and last entries of every row are always **1**. - **Rule 3 — Interior entries:** Each interior number is the **sum of the two numbers directly above it** (left and right parents). $$ \binom{n}{k} = \binom{n-1}{k-1} + \binom{n-1}{k} $$ - **Rule 4 — Row sum:** The sum of all entries in row $n$ is $2^n$. - **Rule 5 — Symmetry:** The triangle is symmetric about its vertical centerline: $\binom{n}{k} = \binom{n}{n-k}$. ### 2.3 Why Pascal's Triangle Matters in FEM > [!NOTE] > In FEM, when we approximate the field variable $u(x)$ as a polynomial of degree $n$, we write: > $$u(x) \approx a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + \dots + a_n x^n$$ > The coefficients $a_0, a_1, \dots, a_n$ are linearly related to the unknown nodal values $u_1, u_2, \dots, u_{n+1}$. The Pascal's Triangle essentially provides a **pre-computed lookup table** for the binomial coefficients that appear when we transform between the **monomial basis** $\{1, x, x^2, \dots, x^n\}$ and the **nodal value basis** $\{u_1, u_2, \dots, u_{n+1}\}$. ### 2.4 Lagrange Polynomial — The Mathematical Engine The **Lagrange Interpolation Polynomial** of degree $n$ passing through $n+1$ distinct nodes $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_{n+1}$ with values $u_1, u_2, \dots, u_{n+1}$ is: $$ u(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n+1} N_i(x) \, u_i, \quad \text{where} \quad N_i(x) = \prod_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n+1} \frac{x - x_j}{x_i - x_j} $$ The function $N_i(x)$ is the **i-th Lagrange Shape Function**. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Element Type | Nodes | Degree | Shape Function $N_i(x)$ | Natural Coord. $\xi$ Nodes | |:------------:|:-----:|:------:|:------------------------|:--------------------------:| | **Linear (2-node)** | 2 | 1 | $N_1 = \dfrac{x_2 - x}{x_2 - x_1}$, $\quad N_2 = \dfrac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1}$ | $\xi = -1, +1$ | | **Quadratic (3-node)** | 3 | 2 | $N_1 = \dfrac{(x - x_2)(x - x_3)}{(x_1 - x_2)(x_1 - x_3)}$ | $\xi = -1, 0, +1$ | | | | | $N_2 = \dfrac{(x - x_1)(x - x_3)}{(x_2 - x_1)(x_2 - x_3)}$ | | | | | | $N_3 = \dfrac{(x - x_1)(x - x_2)}{(x_3 - x_1)(x_3 - x_2)}$ | | | **Cubic (4-node)** | 4 | 3 | $N_i = \prod_{j \neq i} \dfrac{x - x_j}{x_i - x_j}$, $\quad i = 1,2,3,4$ | $\xi = -1, -\tfrac{1}{3}, +\tfrac{1}{3}, +1$ | | Property | Mathematical Statement | Significance | |:---------|:----------------------|:-------------| | **Kronecker Delta** | $N_i(x_j) = \delta_{ij}$ | Ensures $u(x_i) = u_i$ exactly. | | **Partition of Unity** | $\sum N_i(x) = 1$ | Guarantees rigid-body motion is preserved. | | **Compact Support** | $N_i \neq 0$ only inside element containing node $i$ | Localizes the FE approximation. | | **Polynomial Completeness** | $\sum u_i = $ constant $\Rightarrow u = $ constant | Linear fields are reproduced exactly. | | **Completeness Order** | Sum includes constant + linear $\Rightarrow$ order 1 | Higher polynomial fields need higher-order elements. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Shape functions constructed via Lagrange interpolation are the **backbone of 1-D bar, beam, truss, and heat conduction elements**; in higher dimensions (2-D, 3-D), they are extended using **tensor products** of 1-D Lagrange functions (e.g., bilinear 4-node quad, biquadratic 9-node quad, tricubic 27-node brick). ### 2.6 Worked Connection — Pascal's Triangle & Shape Function Coefficients For a 3-node quadratic element, the displacement field is: $$ u(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 $$ Writing this in matrix form with the monomial basis vector $\{1, x, x^2\}$ and using Pascal's coefficients $\{1, 2, 1\}$ as the transformation weights, we get a $3 \times 3$ coefficient matrix $C$: $$ C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -x_1 & x_1^2 \\ 1 & -x_2 & x_2^2 \\ 1 & -x_3 & x_3^2 \end{bmatrix}^{-1} \quad \text{(where } \{1, 2, 1\} \text{ motifs appear in } C \text{ for equally spaced nodes).} $$ This matrix $C$ is precisely what converts the monomial coefficients $(a_0, a_1, a_2)$ into the nodal values $(u_1, u_2, u_3)$ — **and back**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pascal's Triangle Row $n = 4$ from First Principles **Step 1:** Apply the binomial theorem for $n = 4$: $$ (1 + x)^4 = \sum_{k=0}^{4} \binom{4}{k} x^k $$ **Step 2:** Expand the summation explicitly: $$ (1 + x)^4 = \binom{4}{0} x^0 + \binom{4}{1} x^1 + \binom{4}{2} x^2 + \binom{4}{3} x^3 + \binom{4}{4} x^4 $$ **Step 3:** Evaluate each binomial coefficient using $\binom{n}{k} = \dfrac{n!}{k!(n-k)!}$: - $\binom{4}{0} = \dfrac{4!}{0! \cdot 4!} = 1$ - $\binom{4}{1} = \dfrac{4!}{1! \cdot 3!} = 4$ - $\binom{4}{2} = \dfrac{4!}{2! \cdot 2!} = 6$ - $\binom{4}{3} = \dfrac{4!}{3! \cdot 1!} = 4$ - $\binom{4}{4} = \dfrac{4!}{4! \cdot 0!} = 1$ **Step 4:** Assemble the polynomial and write the triangle row: $$ (1 + x)^4 = 1 + 4x + 6x^2 + 4x^3 + x^4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Row 4: } 1, 4, 6, 4, 1 $$ **Step 5:** Cross-verify using the recursive rule: each entry equals sum of two parents above it. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shape Functions for a 2-Node Linear Bar Element (Cartesian) **Given:** Two nodes at positions $x_1$ and $x_2$ with nodal displacements $u_1$ and $u_2$. **Step 1:** Assume a linear field: $u(x) = a_0 + a_1 x$. **Step 2:** Apply nodal conditions: $$ u_1 = a_0 + a_1 x_1 \quad \text{and} \quad u_2 = a_0 + a_1 x_2 $$ **Step 3:** Write in matrix form: $$ \begin{bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x_1 \\ 1 & x_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 4:** Invert the coefficient matrix. The determinant is $x_2 - x_1$, giving: $$ \begin{bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{x_2 - x_1} \begin{bmatrix} x_2 & -x_1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 5:** Substitute back into $u(x) = a_0 + a_1 x$ and group by nodal values: $$ u(x) = \underbrace{\left( \frac{x_2 - x}{x_2 - x_1} \right)}_{N_1(x)} u_1 + \underbrace{\left( \frac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1} \right)}_{N_2(x)} u_2 $$ **Step 6:** Verify the **Kronecker Delta** property: - $N_1(x_1) = \dfrac{x_2 - x_1}{x_2 - x_1} = 1$ ✓ - $N_1(x_2) = \dfrac{x_2 - x_2}{x_2 - x_1} = 0$ ✓ - $N_2(x_1) = \dfrac{x_1 - x_1}{x_2 - x_1} = 0$ ✓ - $N_2(x_2) = \dfrac{x_2 - x_1}{x_2 - x_1} = 1$ ✓ **Step 7:** Verify the **Partition of Unity**: $$ N_1(x) + N_2(x) = \frac{x_2 - x}{x_2 - x_1} + \frac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \frac{x_2 - x_1}{x_2 - x_1} = 1 \quad \checkmark $$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shape Functions for a 3-Node Quadratic Bar Element (Cartesian) **Given:** Three equally-spaced nodes at $x_1$, $x_2 = \dfrac{x_1 + x_3}{2}$, $x_3$. **Step 1:** Assume quadratic field: $u(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2$. **Step 2:** Apply the general Lagrange formula for $i = 1, 2, 3$: $$ N_i(x) = \prod_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{3} \frac{x - x_j}{x_i - x_j} $$ **Step 3:** Expand for $N_1(x)$: $$ N_1(x) = \frac{(x - x_2)(x - x_3)}{(x_1 - x_2)(x_1 - x_3)} $$ **Step 4:** Expand for $N_2(x)$: $$ N_2(x) = \frac{(x - x_1)(x - x_3)}{(x_2 - x_1)(x_2 - x_3)} $$ **Step 5:** Expand for $N_3(x)$: $$ N_3(x) = \frac{(x - x_1)(x - x_2)}{(x_3 - x_1)(x_3 - x_2)} $$ **Step 6:** Substituting equally spaced nodes $x_1 = 0$, $x_2 = L/2$, $x_3 = L$ and simplifying: $$ N_1(x) = \frac{(2x - L)(x - L)}{L^2} \cdot 2 = 1 - \frac{3x}{L} + \frac{2x^2}{L^2} $$ $$ N_2(x) = \frac{x(x - L)}{(L/2)(-L/2)} = \frac{4x(L - x)}{L^2} = \frac{4x}{L} - \frac{4x^2}{L^2} $$ $$ N_3(x) = \frac{x(2x - L)}{L^2} = -\frac{x}{L} + \frac{2x^2}{L^2} $$ **Step 7:** **Pascal's Triangle Verification:** The coefficients of $\{1, x, x^2\}$ in $(N_1, N_2, N_3)$ are: $$ \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -3/L & 2/L^2 \\ 0 & 4/L & -4/L^2 \\ 0 & -1/L & 2/L^2 \end{bmatrix} \quad \longleftarrow \text{column sums follow Pascal pattern } 1, 2, 1 $$ The sum $N_1 + N_2 + N_3 = 1$ ✓ (Partition of Unity). ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Shape Functions in Natural Coordinate $\xi \in [-1, 1]$ (2-Node Linear Element) **Step 1:** Map Cartesian to natural coordinates using the linear transformation: $$ x(\xi) = \frac{(1 - \xi) x_1 + (1 + \xi) x_2}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \xi = \frac{2x - (x_1 + x_2)}{x_2 - x_1} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute $x(\xi)$ into $N_1(x)$: $$ N_1(\xi) = \frac{x_2 - x(\xi)}{x_2 - x_1} = \frac{1 - \xi}{2} $$ **Step 3:** Substitute into $N_2(x)$: $$ N_2(\xi) = \frac{x(\xi) - x_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \frac{1 + \xi}{2} $$ **Step 4:** Final compact result: $$ N_1(\xi) = \frac{1 - \xi}{2}, \qquad N_2(\xi) = \frac{1 + \xi}{2} $$ ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Shape Functions in Natural Coordinate $\xi$ (3-Node Quadratic Element) For nodes at $\xi_1 = -1$, $\xi_2 = 0$, $\xi_3 = +1$: $$ N_1(\xi) = \frac{(\xi - 0)(\xi - 1)}{(-1 - 0)(-1 - 1)} = \frac{\xi(\xi - 1)}{2} $$ $$ N_2(\xi) = \frac{(\xi + 1)(\xi - 1)}{(0 + 1)(0 - 1)} = \frac{1 - \xi^2}{1} = 1 - \xi^2 $$ $$ N_3(\xi) = \frac{(\xi + 1)(\xi - 0)}{(1 + 1)(1 - 0)} = \frac{\xi(\xi + 1)}{2} $$ **Final compact results:** $$ N_1(\xi) = \frac{\xi(\xi - 1)}{2}, \quad N_2(\xi) = (1 - \xi^2), \quad N_3(\xi) = \frac{\xi(\xi + 1)}{2} $$ ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix Summary | Coordinate System | Element | $\mathbf{N_1}$ | $\mathbf{N_2}$ | $\mathbf{N_3}$ | $\mathbf{N_4}$ | |:------------------|:-------:|:---------------:|:---------------:|:---------------:|:---------------:| | **Cartesian $x$** | Linear | $\dfrac{x_2 - x}{x_2 - x_1}$ | $\dfrac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1}$ | — | — | | **Natural $\xi$** | Linear | $\dfrac{1 - \xi}{2}$ | $\dfrac{1 + \xi}{2}$ | — | — | | **Cartesian $x$** | Quadratic | $\dfrac{(x - x_2)(x - x_3)}{(x_1 - x_2)(x_1 - x_3)}$ | $\dfrac{(x - x_1)(x - x_3)}{(x_2 - x_1)(x_2 - x_3)}$ | $\dfrac{(x - x_1)(x - x_2)}{(x_3 - x_1)(x_3 - x_2)}$ | — | | **Natural $\xi$** | Quadratic | $\dfrac{\xi(\xi - 1)}{2}$ | $1 - \xi^2$ | $\dfrac{\xi(\xi + 1)}{2}$ | — | | **Natural $\xi$** | Cubic | $-\dfrac{9}{16}(\xi + \tfrac{1}{3})(\xi - \tfrac{1}{3})(\xi - 1)$ | $\dfrac{27}{16}(\xi + 1)(\xi - \tfrac{1}{3})(\xi - 1)$ | $-\dfrac{27}{16}(\xi + 1)(\xi + \tfrac{1}{3})(\xi - 1)$ | $\dfrac{9}{16}(\xi + 1)(\xi + \tfrac{1}{3})(\xi - \tfrac{1}{3})$ | # 4.
